data.history: The Al Roumi family represents a diverse lineage with documented branches across the Levant and Gulf regions. The Lebanese branch, exemplified by Majida El Roumi's lineage, traces to her father Halim El Roumi, a Melkite Christian born in Tyre, South Lebanon, whose family migrated to Haifa in 1921 to escape the hardships of World War I. In Kuwait, the Al Roumi name appears through Ahmad Al-Bishr Al-Roumi, a scholar and compiler who worked closely with the renowned Kuwaiti poet Saqr Al-Shubayb in the mid-20th century. The UAE branch is represented by Mariam Mohammed Khalfan Al Roumi, born February 2, 1957, who became a prominent political figure serving as Minister of Social Affairs from 2006-2014 under Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid's government. These separate branches appear to represent distinct family lines rather than a unified genealogy, with the Lebanese branch being Christian and the Gulf branches likely following different religious and tribal affiliations.
data.description: Al Roumi is a family name found across multiple Arab regions, with notable documented branches in Lebanon (Christian Melkite origins), Kuwait (scholarly tradition), and the UAE (political prominence), though these appear to represent separate lineages rather than a unified family tree.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence across different sectors: in Lebanon through cultural and artistic achievements (Majida El Roumi as internationally recognized soprano), in Kuwait through scholarly contributions (Ahmad Al-Bishr Al-Roumi's literary work), and in the UAE through political leadership (Mariam Al Roumi's ministerial service and social development work).

data.history: The research provided contains extensive information about the name Muhammad and its variants, the term Al-Fadl, and the Islamic State organization, but contains no specific information about the Al Fadl Dynasty as a family or tribal entity. While there are references to historical figures named Al-Fadl ibn various patronymics who served as governors and officials in the Abbasid Caliphate (8th-9th centuries CE), and mention of 'Al Fadl' as an Arab dynasty that ruled parts of Syria, no detailed information is provided about this dynasty's origins, migration patterns, or family structure. The research also mentions Fadl ibn Rabi'ah (ca. 1107) as a Bedouin emir and progenitor of 'the Al Fadl dynasty', and Fadl ibn Isa (13th century) as an Al Fadl ruler under the Mamluks, but provides no comprehensive historical narrative about the family's development, territorial control, or modern status.

data.history: The Khashoggi family has Turkish origins, with ancestors making the Hajj pilgrimage from Kayseri to Mecca approximately four centuries ago and deciding to remain in the Arabian Peninsula. The family surname 'Khashoggi' (kaşıkçı in Turkish) means 'spoon maker,' reflecting their Turkish ancestral profession. The family patriarch Muhammad Khashoggi (1889-1978) became a prominent medical doctor and served as personal physician to Saudi Arabia's founder, King Abdulaziz Al Saud, establishing the family's influential position within Saudi society. His children achieved remarkable prominence across multiple spheres: Adnan Khashoggi became one of the world's most notorious arms dealers and businessmen, while his daughter Samira Khashoggi was a novelist and mother to Dodi Fayed. The family maintained strong connections to both Saudi royalty and international business networks, with members serving in various governmental, journalistic, and commercial roles throughout the 20th and 21st centuries.
data.description: A prominent Saudi family of Turkish origin whose ancestors were spoon makers from Kayseri who settled in Arabia centuries ago, achieving significant influence in medicine, journalism, arms dealing, and international business.
data.originStory: The Khashoggi family's Turkish ancestors made the Hajj pilgrimage from the city of Kayseri to Mecca approximately four centuries ago and decided to stay in the Arabian Peninsula rather than return to Turkey.
data.modernStatus: The family remains influential in Saudi society and international business, though some members like journalist Jamal Khashoggi became critics of the Saudi government. The family has connections to arms dealing, journalism, literature, and entertainment industries.
data.nameEtymology: The surname Khashoggi derives from the Turkish word 'kaşıkçı' meaning 'spoon maker,' reflecting their ancestral profession in Turkey.

migrationPath.description: Turkish ancestors made Hajj pilgrimage from Kayseri to Mecca and decided to settle permanently in Arabia

data.history: The Al Qawasem (also spelled Al Qasimi) is an Arab dynasty and tribe of Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. The Qawasem are Huwala Arabs whose ancestors migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, maintaining rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province until its annexation by Iran in 1887. During the 18th century, they embraced the Wahhabi/Muwahhidun movement and became close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, the Qawasem had emerged as a powerful maritime confederation controlling trade routes in the Persian Gulf, with strongholds in Ras Al Khaimah on the southern shore and Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh on the northern shore. Their control of Gulf trade led to conflicts with the British-allied Omani Empire over border disputes, religious differences, and naval dominance. Beginning in 1804, there was a spike in Qawasem naval attacks on trading ships, leading the British to label them as 'pirates' and the southern Gulf shore as the 'Pirate Coast.' The British mounted two major military campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819, with the latter resulting in the General Maritime Treaty of 1820 that established the Trucial States system. Today, the Al Qawasem rule two of the seven UAE emirates: Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, making them one of the longest reigning royal families in the Arabian Peninsula.
data.description: The Al Qawasem (Al Qasimi) is a Hashemite Arab dynasty and tribal confederation that historically controlled maritime trade in the Persian Gulf from both Arabian and Persian shores. They currently rule the UAE emirates of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah.
data.originStory: The Qawasem tribe is of Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. They are Huwala Arabs whose ancestors migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, maintaining connections and rulership on both sides of the Gulf.
data.modernStatus: Currently rule two of the seven UAE emirates: Sharjah (under Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi) and Ras Al Khaimah (under Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi). Both rulers are members of the UAE Supreme Council.
data.nameEtymology: Al Qawasem is the plural form of Al Qasimi, derived from their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. The British referred to them archaically as 'Joasmee.'

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Anqari family appears to be referenced in Saudi media contexts, with Al Jawhara bint Mohammed Al Anqari noted as a pioneering deputy chair of the National Society for Human Rights who writes for Okaz newspaper. Another reference appears in connection with Moudie bint Abdul Mohsen Al Angari, who was the third wife of Prince Talal bin Abdulaziz Al Saud and mother to Prince Turki bin Talal and Princess Sara bint Talal Al Saud. Moudie died in 2008, and her daughter Sara later sought political asylum in the United Kingdom in 2012. data.history:
The Al Mubarak family appears to be a modern designation referring to descendants and branches related to Mubarak Al-Sabah (c. 1837-1915), the seventh ruler of Kuwait. Mubarak Al-Sabah, known as 'Mubarak the Great' and nicknamed 'The lion of the peninsula,' was born into Kuwait's powerful Al-Sabah ruling family in 1837 as the son of Sheikh Sabah II Al-Sabah. He ascended to power in 1896 after assassinating his predecessor and half-brother Muhammad Al-Sabah, along with another brother Jarrah, in a dramatic power struggle on May 8, 1896.

Mubarak's reign (1896-1915) was transformative for Kuwait's modern development. He signed the crucial Anglo-Kuwaiti Treaty with Great Britain on November 23, 1899, establishing British protection and founding the basis of modern Kuwait. This agreement required Kuwait not to receive foreign agents or cede territory without British approval. Despite initial Ottoman resistance to his rule, Mubarak eventually gained recognition as kaymakam (sub-governor) in December 1897. His military campaigns, including a disastrous 1901 invasion of Najd that ended in defeat at the Battle of Sarif, marked both his ambitions and limitations as a regional leader.

The Al Mubarak branches within the Al-Sabah family stem from Mubarak's sons, particularly through Hamad Al-Mubarak Al-Sabah (1894-1938), who became the progenitor of the Al-Hamad branch. Hamad was described as his father's favorite child and was one of three candidates nominated to potentially succeed Sheikh Salem Al-Mubarak Al-Sabah. Modern descendants include notable figures like Sheikh Mubarak Abdullah Al-Mubarak Al-Sabah, a contemporary entrepreneur and son of Abdullah Mubarak Al-Sabah (Deputy Emir in the 1950s), who founded Action Hotels and other business ventures. The constitutional requirement that Kuwait's Emir must be a descendant of Mubarak ensures the continuing political significance of his lineage.
data.description: Al Mubarak refers to the descendants and branches of Mubarak Al-Sabah (1837-1915), the transformative seventh ruler of Kuwait who established British protection and founded modern Kuwait. His lineage continues through various Al-Sabah family branches, including contemporary business and political figures.
data.originStory: The Al Mubarak designation stems from Mubarak Al-Sabah, who was born into Kuwait's Al-Sabah ruling family around 1837. The Al-Sabah family originated from the Bani Utbah confederation and settled in Kuwait around the mid-1700s after being expelled from Umm Qasr in southern Iraq by the Ottomans due to their predatory activities against caravans and trading ships.
data.modernStatus: The Al Mubarak branches remain integral to Kuwait's Al-Sabah ruling family, with constitutional requirements ensuring Kuwaiti Emirs must be Mubarak's descendants. Contemporary members include entrepreneurs like Sheikh Mubarak Abdullah Al-Mubarak Al-Sabah (founder of Action Hotels, Action Real Estate) and political figures throughout Kuwait's government hierarchy.

folkLegends.title: The Night Assassination
folkLegends.story: On May 8, 1896, Mubarak Al-Sabah, with his sons Jabir and Salim and loyal supporters, secretly entered his half-brother Muhammad's house during the night and assassinated both Muhammad and Jarrah Al-Sabah, seizing the Kuwaiti throne.

data.nameEtymology: Mubarak is an Arabic name meaning 'blessed' or 'who is blessed,' derived from the Semitic root B-R-K meaning 'to kneel, bless.' The name is the Arabic equivalent of the Latinate 'Benedict' and is cognate with the Hebrew 'Baruch.'

data.history: The Al Mishari family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Gulf region, with no verified connection between them based on available research. The most prominent branch is part of the House of Saud's cadet branches, descended from Mishari, one of the brothers of Muhammad bin Saud (the 18th century founder of the Saudi state). This branch holds the surname 'Al Saud' as descendants of the dynasty's founding generation, though they are not in the direct line of succession to the Saudi throne. Prince Mishari bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1932-2000) was a notable member of this branch, serving as a businessman and poet who owned Al Saada Trading and Contracting in Jeddah. His children include Prince Mohammed bin Mishari, who serves on the Allegiance Council, and Princess Maha bint Mishari, a prominent physician and academic. A completely separate Al Mishari lineage appears in Kuwait with Qari Mishary bin Rashid Alafasy, a renowned Quran reciter and imam who has gained international recognition for his religious work, though no tribal or family connections to other Al Mishari branches are documented in the available research.
data.description: The Al Mishari represents multiple unconnected family lineages, including a Saudi royal cadet branch descended from the House of Saud's founding generation and a separate Kuwaiti religious family, with no verified tribal connections between the branches.
data.tribalOrigin: Banu Hanifa (for Saudi branch only - as part of broader House of Saud origins)
data.modernStatus: Saudi branch holds positions in government and business, with members serving on the Allegiance Council and in academic/medical fields. Kuwaiti branch prominent in Islamic scholarship and religious education.

data.history: The Al Neyadi family appears to have established roots in the Umm Ghafa area, approximately 30 kilometers southeast of Al Ain in Abu Dhabi. The most prominent documented member is Sultan Saif Al Neyadi, born on May 23, 1981, in Umm Ghafa, who has achieved international recognition as the UAE's first Arab astronaut to serve on the International Space Station and perform a spacewalk. The family has also produced notable figures in the arts, with Alaya Al Neyadi becoming the UAE's first professional Emirati ballet dancer. Her mother, Svetlana Al Neyadi, a Ukrainian ballet teacher, established the Fantasia Ballet school in Abu Dhabi in 1997, indicating the family's involvement in cultural development. The Al Neyadi family's modern prominence spans multiple sectors including space exploration, military service, government positions, and performing arts, with Sultan Al Neyadi serving as UAE Minister of State for Youth Affairs since January 2024.
data.description: An Emirati family based in the Abu Dhabi region, prominently known for producing Sultan Al Neyadi, the UAE's pioneering astronaut and current Minister of State for Youth Affairs, and contributions to the performing arts through ballet.
data.modernStatus: The family holds significant positions in UAE government and cultural sectors, with Sultan Al Neyadi serving as UAE Minister of State for Youth Affairs and having achieved historic milestones as an astronaut, while family members contribute to the arts and cultural education sector.

data.history: The Al Muhailan family appears to have connections to both Jordan and Kuwait based on the available research. In Jordan, Mahmoud Muhailan served as Assistant Regional Secretary of the Arab Ba'ath Progressive Party, a small political organization that operated as the Jordanian regional branch of the Syrian-led Ba'ath Party from its legal registration in 1993 until its dissolution in 2023. The party was led by Fuad Dabbour as Regional Secretary, with Mahmoud Muhailan in the deputy position. Additionally, research indicates an M. Muhailan collaborated on medical research published in Jordan, co-authoring a study on computerized tomography of urinary stones published in Dirasaat journal in 1985 alongside W. Almaani and M. Karmi. This suggests the family has maintained presence in academic or professional circles in Jordan for several decades.

data.history: The Al Arayed family appears to be connected to the Al-Abdali dynasty that ruled the Sultanate of Lahej in southern Yemen for over two centuries. Based on the research about Ahmed Fadhl al-Qumindan (Ahmed Fadl bin Ali bin Muhsin Al-Abdali), the Al-Abdali family established authority in Lahij and Aden as a sheikhdom in the 1730s following political upheaval after the dissolution of the First Ottoman Empire in Yemen. By the early nineteenth century, the Abdali Sultanate had become an ally of the British colonial powers in southern Yemen and was incorporated into the Aden Protectorate. The family's tribal origins are disputed - while some sources trace them to various Yemeni tribes, Ahmed Fadl al-Qumindan argued in his historical work that the Abdali lineage traces to the Kalid tribe, one of the Yafa'a tribes, though he noted that the family's connection to their tribal origins had long been severed. The family experienced significant upheaval during World War I when Ottoman forces occupied Lahij in 1915, forcing the Abdali family into exile in Aden under British protection until the Ottomans withdrew in 1918. The sultanate continued under British influence until the mid-20th century, with the family maintaining considerable agricultural wealth and political influence in the region.
data.description: The Al Arayed family appears connected to the Al-Abdali dynasty that ruled the Sultanate of Lahej in southern Yemen for over two centuries, serving as British allies in the Aden Protectorate. The family maintained significant political and economic influence through agricultural holdings and military leadership roles.
data.originStory: According to Ahmed Fadl al-Qumindan's historical research, the Al-Abdali family traces its lineage to the Kalid tribe, one of the Yafa'a tribes, though this origin is disputed by other sources that link them to various other Yemeni tribal groups including Lahij tribes, Arhab near Sana'a, or Bakil tribe.
data.modernStatus: Historical ruling family of the former Sultanate of Lahej, with documented agricultural and cultural contributions through the mid-20th century. Current status unclear from available research.

data.history: The Al Matrook family gained tragic prominence during the 2011 Bahraini uprising when Fadhel Salman Ali Salman Al-Matrook became the second fatality of the protests. Born on November 8, 1979, Fadhel was a 31-year-old unemployed Bahraini who lived in his father's house after his father died when he was 8 years old. He was married with two children - a 5-year-old son and a 2-year-old daughter - and had been waiting seven years for housing assistance from the government at the time of his death. On February 15, 2011, during the funeral procession of Ali Abdulhadi Mushaima (the first victim of the Day of Rage), Fadhel was shot and killed by Bahraini security forces who fired birdshot pellets at close range (2-5 meters) while he was trying to help maintain peace between mourners and riot police. According to eyewitness accounts, Al-Matrook was part of a human chain formed to prevent clashes when security forces opened fire indiscriminately after a provocateur threw a stone at police. He was shot in the back and died in Salmaniya Medical Complex from internal bleeding and punctured lungs. His death sparked further protests and became a symbol of the government's violent crackdown on peaceful demonstrators during the Arab Spring in Bahrain.
data.description: A Bahraini family that became tragically prominent during the 2011 uprising when Fadhel Al-Matrook was killed by security forces during a peaceful funeral procession, becoming the second fatality of the Bahraini protests.
data.modernStatus: The family gained recognition primarily through the tragic death of Fadhel Al-Matrook during the 2011 Bahraini uprising, with his case becoming part of investigations into government use of excessive force against protesters.

data.history: The Al Muraikhi family appears prominently in contemporary Qatari politics and diplomacy, with Sultan bin Saad bin Sultan Al Muraikhi currently serving as Qatar's Minister of State for Foreign Affairs. The family name gained international recognition during the 2023 Paraguay honor ceremony, when Sultan Al Muraikhi received the National Order of Merit on behalf of Qatar's Prime Minister Mohammed bin Abdulrahman Al Thani, demonstrating the family's trusted diplomatic role. During the 148th Session of the Arab League Council in Cairo, Sultan Al Muraikhi, representing Qatar as State Minister for Foreign Affairs, engaged in heated diplomatic exchanges regarding Qatar's relationship with Iran and Saudi Arabia during the 2017-2021 diplomatic crisis. The family has also been associated with Qatar's cultural development, with Hessa Al-Muraikhi noted as a supervisor of women's art education sections in Qatar's artistic development initiatives of the 1980s. The research indicates the Al Muraikhi family has maintained significant positions in Qatar's modern governmental structure, particularly in foreign affairs and cultural sectors.

The family has played important diplomatic roles during Qatar's recent international crises and cultural development initiatives.

data.history: The Al Sudairi family is one of the most influential noble families in Saudi Arabia, part of the Dawasir tribe and based in the Najd region. The family's political prominence began with Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Turki Al Sudairi (1869–1936), who was a powerful chief of the Sudairi tribe and one of the early supporters of King Abdulaziz during his conquest of Saudi Arabia. Following the formation of the state, Ahmed served as governor in multiple provinces including Washm, Sudair, Qassim, and Aflaj, establishing the family's administrative influence. The Al Sudairi family's most significant contribution to Saudi history came through marriages with the Al Saud royal family. Sara bint Ahmed Al Sudairi (c. 1860–1910) was the mother of King Abdulaziz, the founder of Saudi Arabia. Later, her relative Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi (1900–1969) became one of King Abdulaziz's wives and mother to the famous 'Sudairi Seven' - seven sons including future kings Fahd and Salman, as well as crown princes Sultan and Nayef. The Sudairi Seven became the most powerful faction within the Saudi royal family, controlling key government positions for decades. Through strategic marriages and political appointments, the Al Sudairi family has maintained its influence across multiple generations, with members serving as governors of various provinces and their descendants continuing to hold important positions in the modern Saudi state.
data.description: The Al Sudairi is a prominent noble family from the Najd region of Saudi Arabia, part of the Dawasir tribe, known for their crucial political alliance with the Al Saud royal family and their role in producing the influential 'Sudairi Seven' sons of King Abdulaziz.
data.originStory: The Al Sudairi family are part of the noble Dawasir tribe, settled in Al Ghat, an oasis town located in central Arabia nearly 250 kilometers northwest of Riyadh. They established themselves as a powerful tribal confederation in the Najd region.
data.modernStatus: The Al Sudairi family maintains significant influence in Saudi Arabia through their descendants in the royal family. King Salman (currently reigning) and Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman represent the continuation of Sudairi influence. Various family branches continue to hold governorships and important positions in the Saudi government.

data.history: Based on available evidence, the Al Jedaie family is documented as a business family operating in Saudi Arabia, specifically through their involvement in major commercial real estate ventures. The Al-Jedaie Group formed a significant partnership with the Al-Hokair Group to establish the Khaldia Towers Company, which owns one of Riyadh's most prominent landmarks. The partnership acquired and developed the Khaldia Towers project, a 33-floor mixed-use skyscraper built between 1976 and 1983 in Riyadh's ad-Dirah neighborhood. This development was constructed on land donated by the sons of King Khalid bin Abdulaziz from his former palace site in Hotat Khalid, indicating the Al Jedaie family's access to high-level business opportunities and significant capital for major real estate investments in the Saudi capital.
data.description: Al Jedaie is a Saudi business family known for their involvement in major real estate development through the Al-Jedaie Group, particularly their partnership in owning Khaldia Towers in Riyadh.
data.modernStatus: The Al-Jedaie Group operates as a significant business entity in Saudi Arabia, maintaining a partnership with Al-Hokair Group through Khaldia Towers Company, which owns major commercial real estate properties in Riyadh including the landmark Four Points by Sheraton Riyadh Khaldia.

data.history: The Batwa are a Bantu-speaking indigenous pygmy people native to the African Great Lakes region, traditionally semi-nomadic hunter-gatherers of mountain forests. When the Hutu people arrived in the region, they subjugated the 'bush people' they called Abatwa, generally assumed to be ancestors of today's Twa, though the exact relationship remains unclear. For several hundred years, the Twa have been a small minority (currently 1% in Rwanda and Burundi), with little political role, though some obtained privileged positions in royal courts as entertainers or executioners. Unusually for Pygmies, the Twa developed pottery skills. Due to forest clearing for agriculture, logging, development projects, and conservation areas, the Twa have been forced from their ancestral lands without compensation or recognition of land rights. Most are now landless and live in poverty, with limited access to education and government representation. In 1975, some 6,000 Batwa were expelled from Kahuzi-Biega National Park; after failed negotiations, they returned in 2019 but faced violent suppression from park authorities.
data.description: The Batwa are an indigenous Bantu-speaking pygmy people of the African Great Lakes region, traditionally forest hunter-gatherers who have been displaced from ancestral lands and now live mostly in poverty. They are known for their pottery skills and numbered approximately 80,000 people across Rwanda, Burundi, Uganda and eastern Democratic Republic of Congo as of 2000.
data.originStory: The Batwa are generally assumed to be the oldest surviving population of the Great Lakes region. When Bantu-speaking Hutu people arrived, they subjugated indigenous 'bush people' called Abatwa, though it's unclear whether modern Twa are direct descendants of these original inhabitants, arrived alongside the Hutu as a distinct people, or have mixed ancestry.
data.modernStatus: The Batwa remain marginalized with no recognized land rights, living primarily as landless minorities in Rwanda, Burundi, Uganda and eastern DRC. Some individuals have achieved political representation, including senators like Vital Bambanze and Charles Masabo in Burundi, and politicians like Adolphine Muley in Congo.
data.nameEtymology: Also known as Abatwa or Ge-Sera in addition to Batwa (singular Mutwa)

data.history: The Al Albani family's most prominent member was Muhammad Nasiruddin al-Albani (1914-1999), an Albanian Islamic hadith scholar who became one of the most influential figures of modern Salafism. Born in Shkodër, Albania, his family migrated to Damascus, Syria in 1923 when he was nine years old, fleeing the rise of secularism under King Zog I. His father, Nuh Nexhati, was a Hanafi jurist trained in Istanbul who feared the secular policies of the Albanian government. In Damascus, al-Albani received his religious education and became known as 'al-Albani' (the Albanian) after leaving formal schooling. He initially worked as a carpenter and later as a watchmaker with his father before dedicating himself to Islamic scholarship. Al-Albani developed into a controversial yet influential Islamic reformer who challenged traditional schools of jurisprudence and advocated for direct interpretation of Quran and hadith. He taught at the Islamic University of Madinah at the invitation of Ibn Baz and authored over 200 works on hadith and Islamic jurisprudence. Despite facing imprisonment twice in Syria for his teachings, he gained recognition among Salafi scholars as 'al-Bukhari of the contemporary age' for his rigorous re-evaluation of hadith literature. He eventually settled in Jordan where he remained until his death in 1999.
data.description: The Al Albani family traces to Albanian origins, with their most notable member being Muhammad Nasiruddin al-Albani (1914-1999), a prominent Islamic scholar who migrated from Albania to Syria in 1923 and became a leading figure in modern Salafism.
data.originStory: The family originated in Albania, specifically from Shkodër. Muhammad Nasiruddin al-Albani was born there in 1914 to a family adhering to the Hanafi school of Islamic jurisprudence. His father, Nuh Nexhati, was a trained Islamic jurist who had studied in Istanbul and served as a religious authority in their community.
data.modernStatus: The Al Albani family's influence is primarily through the scholarly legacy of Muhammad Nasiruddin al-Albani, whose works continue to be studied in Salafi circles worldwide. His descendants and followers maintain his religious teachings and publications.
data.nameEtymology: The surname 'al-Albani' means 'the Albanian,' which Muhammad Nasiruddin adopted after leaving formal schooling in Damascus, identifying him by his ethnic and geographical origin from Albania.

migrationPath.description: Family migration led by Nuh Nexhati fleeing secularization under King Zog I of Albania

data.history: The Al Qarni family traces its origins to the Qarn region of Saudi Arabia, with historical connections to the ancient Qarani tribe. The name appears in early Islamic history through Uways ibn Amir al-Qarani, a revered 7th century figure who was a companion of Ali and died at the Battle of Siffin in 657 CE. Modern members of the Al Qarni family have emerged as prominent Islamic scholars and public figures in contemporary Saudi Arabia. The family has produced several notable religious scholars and activists, including Dr. Awad ibn Mohammed al-Qarni (born 1959) and Musa al-Qarni (1954-2021), both of whom became influential voices in Islamic scholarship and political reform movements. The family has maintained strong connections to traditional Islamic scholarship while also engaging with contemporary political and social issues, often finding themselves at odds with Saudi government policies in recent decades.
data.description: Al Qarni is a Saudi Arabian family of Islamic scholars and activists, historically connected to the Qarn region and the ancient Qarani tribal lineage, known for producing prominent religious figures and political reformers.
data.originStory: The Al Qarni lineage traces back to the historical Qarani tribe, with Uways ibn Amir al-Qarani serving as an early Islamic figure who never met Prophet Muhammad but was honored by him through hadith. Local traditions in Yemen trace the al-Qarani name to Ibb city, where Uways was reportedly born.
data.tribalOrigin: Qarani (historically connected to Murad tribe sub-branch)
data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al Qarni family members are primarily Islamic scholars and academics, with several facing government persecution for political activism and reform advocacy in Saudi Arabia.

data.history: Based on the limited research available, Al Anezi appears in historical records primarily through Sheikh Hasan bin Ali Al Anezi, who ruled Ras Al Khaimah from 1809-1814 during a turbulent period of British campaigns against the Al Qasimi confederation. This brief rule occurred between two periods of Al Qasimi leadership, suggesting the Al Anezi may have been installed as interim rulers or represented a competing faction during the political upheaval following British military actions in the region. The research indicates that Sheikh Hasan bin Ali Al Anezi's rule came immediately after Sheikh Sultan Bin Saqr Al Qasimi's first period (1803-1809) and before Sheikh Hassan bin Rahma Al Qasimi's rule (1814-1820), placing the Al Anezi leadership during one of the most politically volatile periods in the Trucial States' history. The context suggests their involvement in the broader tribal and political dynamics of early 19th century Ras Al Khaimah, though the specific circumstances of how they came to power and their ultimate fate remain unclear from the available sources.
data.description: Al Anezi appears in historical records as rulers of Ras Al Khaimah from 1809-1814, during the period of British military campaigns against regional maritime powers.

achievements: Ruled Ras Al Khaimah during politically turbulent period

data.history: Based on the research provided, there is no direct information about the Ba Aqeel family specifically. However, the research does reveal connections to several prominent families and individuals with the Aqeel name across different contexts. The House of al-Sagoff (also transliterated as al-Saqqaf) represents a Hadhrami Arab family historically active in Saudi Arabia, Yemen, Jordan, UAE, Malaysia, Indonesia and Singapore, tracing lineage to the Ba'Alawi clan of Banu Hashim in Hadhramaut. One notable member mentioned is Muhammad bin Aqeel Al-Saqqaf, who was a wealthy businessman and political leader ruling over Dhofar, Oman for 25 years, receiving gifts from Muhammad Ali Pasha of Egypt. The research also references Abu Abd al-Rahman Ibn Aqil al-Zahiri, a Saudi Arabian polymath born in Shaqra in 1938, known as a theologian, jurist, historian and author, though his specific family connections are not detailed. Additionally, there are references to various Sufi lineages mentioning individuals with Aqeel in their spiritual chains, particularly in the Qadri tradition where 'Aqeel' appears as a link in the spiritual genealogy tracing back to Abdul-Qadir Gilani.
data.description: The research contains references to individuals with the Aqeel name across different contexts but provides no specific information about the Ba Aqeel family as a distinct genealogical unit.

data.history: The Al Kathiri (Arabic: الكثيري) is a Yemeni tribe based in the south of the Arabian Peninsula, representing one of the largest tribes of Banu Quda'a with historical alliance to Banu Hamdan of Hadramawt. The Al-Kathiri Family formed a natural extension of Banu Hamdan since the pre-Islamic era, establishing the Al-Kathiri Sultanate that extended from Dhofar and Shabwa in the east to the west and north into the Empty Quarter and south to the seashore of the Arabian Sea. The Sultanate controlled most Hadhrami tribes under its protection, with its capital at Seiyoun, though faced rebellions from tribes like Alhmoum, Sheikh Al Amoudi and Yafa. The emergence of the Qu'aiti Sultanate at the beginning of the 19th century with British support led to territorial division, with north and east Hadramout remaining under Al Kathiri control while the west came under Qu'aiti rule. After a century of warfare, both sultanates reconciled and signed a treaty under British superintendence in 1937 at the Sultan Al Kathiri's palace in Seiyoun. The Al-Kathiri Sultanate was officially established in 1379, ruling until 1967 when the 14 October Revolution expelled British colonizers and unified the sultanates into South Yemen. Today, Al Kathiri populations are found across Yemen, Oman, United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and have significant diaspora communities in Southeast Asia and East Africa including Somalia, Kenya, and Tanzania.
data.description: The Al Kathiri is a major Yemeni tribe of Banu Quda'a origin that established a historic sultanate in Hadramawt from 1379-1967. The tribe maintains populations across the GCC states and significant diaspora communities in Southeast Asia and East Africa.
data.originStory: The Al Kathiri tribe originated as one of the largest tribes of Banu Quda'a, forming alliances with Banu Hamdan of Hadramawt in eastern Yemen and Oman. Genetic studies of haplogroup J1 L222.2 suggest that tribes from Quda'ah, including Al Kathiri, are more closely aligned with the Adnanites, challenging earlier claims of Qahtanite origins that arose during the early Islamic period.
data.modernStatus: Al Kathiri populations are dispersed across GCC states including UAE, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Oman, with significant business communities in Southeast Asia. Notable modern figures include Mari al-Kathiri, first prime minister of East Timor, and Indonesian human rights activist Munir Said Thalib, both descendants of Kathiri immigrants.

migrationPath.description: Significant migration of Hadhramis including Al Kathiri to Southeast Asia in 19th and 20th centuries

data.history: The Al Isfahani family traces its origins to Isfahan, Persia (modern-day Iran), with the epithet 'al-Isfahani' being common to the entire family lineage rather than indicating just one individual's birthplace. The most famous member, Abu al-Faraj al-Isfahani (897-967 CE), was a direct descendant of the last Umayyad caliph Marwan II, establishing the family's Arab-Quraysh origins despite their Persian geographical connections. According to Ibn Hazm, some descendants of Marwan b. Muhammad settled in Isfahan, though the earliest documented family history only dates to Ahmad b. al-Haytham, who settled in Samarra between 835-847 CE. The family became well-entrenched in Abbasid elite networks, with members serving as scribes and maintaining connections with viziers, officials, and Talibid notables. By the early 10th century, the family had relocated from Samarra to Baghdad, where they continued their scholarly and administrative activities. The family produced numerous scholars, historians, and religious authorities across different periods, with branches appearing in various parts of the Islamic world including Iraq and Iran.
data.description: A scholarly family of Arab-Quraysh origin with Persian geographical connections, descended from Umayyad caliph Marwan II, known for producing prominent historians, religious scholars, and court officials across the Islamic world.
data.originStory: The Al Isfahani family descends from the last Umayyad caliph Marwan II (691-750 CE). Some of his descendants settled in Isfahan, Persia, giving the family its geographical epithet. The documented family history begins with Ahmad b. al-Haytham, who settled in Samarra between 835-847 CE and led a privileged life there, with his sons becoming well-connected scribes and officials in the Abbasid court.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Isfahani refers to the city of Isfahan on the Iranian plateau, though it appears to be a family epithet rather than indicating individual birthplace

data.history: The Al Unaizah family takes its name from the city of Unaizah in Al-Qassim Province, Saudi Arabia, which has been historically significant since pre-Islamic times. The city was mentioned in poems by Imru' al-Qais and served as an important stopping point for Muslim pilgrims from Mesopotamia and Persia traveling to Makkah. Since 1818, Unaizah has been ruled by the Al Sulaim dynasty (Sons of Sulaiman Al-Zamil), who came to power when Prince Yahia Al-Sulaim killed the Ottoman-appointed governor Abdullah Al-Jamei in 1822. This ruling dynasty continues to govern the city under a written treaty with the Saudi royal family. The city became known as the 'Paris of Najd' due to its architecture and cultural achievements, as noted by traveler Amin al-Rihani. Unaizah was also notable as one of the first cities in Al-Qassim Province and Najd to embrace female education, leading to national debate before gaining acceptance with King Abdulaziz's intervention.
data.description: Al Unaizah refers to the ruling family and residents of Unaizah, the second-largest city in Al-Qassim Province, Saudi Arabia. The city has been governed by the Al Sulaim dynasty since 1818 under a treaty with the Saudi royal family.
data.originStory: The Al Sulaim dynasty came to power in Unaizah when Prince Yahia bin Sulaiman Al Zamil Al-Sulaim killed the Ottoman-appointed governor Abdullah Al-Jamei in 1822, establishing their rule over the city that continues to this day under a written treaty with the Saudi royal family.
data.tribalOrigin: Al Sulaim dynasty from the Subay tribe
data.modernStatus: The Al Sulaim family continues to rule Unaizah as a governorate under the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, maintaining their authority through a written treaty with the Saudi royal family. The city is a major agricultural and tourism center in Al-Qassim Province.
data.nameEtymology: Al Unaizah derives from the city name Unaizah (Arabic: عنيزة ʿUnaizah), though the specific etymology of the city name is not provided in the research.

data.history: Al Qatif is a place-based designation referring to people from the Qatif region in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, one of the oldest settlements in the Arabian Peninsula dating back to 3500 BC. The region was historically known as Al-Khatt and was part of the Eastern Arabia region called Greater Bahrain or Dilmun. Throughout history, various dynasties controlled Qatif including the Qarmatians (899-1071), Uyunids (1071-1253), Usfurids (1253-1440), and Jabrids (1440-1515), before Portuguese conquest in 1515-1520. The Ottomans took control in 1549, followed by periods under Banu Khalid rule and the Saudi states. The region became part of the modern Saudi Kingdom in 1913 under Ibn Saud. Most people in Qatif are Arab Baharna who are the original inhabitants of the region, with very similar culture and dialects to the Baharna of Bahrain, representing the largest concentration of Shia Islam in Saudi Arabia.
data.description: Al Qatif refers to people from the historic Qatif region in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, primarily Arab Baharna who are the indigenous Shia Muslim population of this ancient settlement dating back over 5,000 years.
data.originStory: The people of Qatif are primarily Arab Baharna, described as the original inhabitants of the region with very similar culture and dialects to the Baharna of Bahrain. There are also minorities of bedouins from the tribes of Bani Khalid and Bani Hajer concentrated in specific areas.
data.modernStatus: The Qatif region has a population of 552,442 as of 2022 and is the center of the Shia population in Saudi Arabia. Many residents work in the oil industry with companies like Saudi Aramco, or in related petrochemical industries in nearby cities. The region is known for academic excellence, with students consistently achieving top ranks nationally.
data.nameEtymology: The term Qatif is derived from what translates to 'harvest' or 'grain', signifying the area's past agricultural history. The region was also known by the name Al-Khatt, which survives as the eponym of several well-known local families spelled variously as 'Al-Khatti'.

data.history: The Al Turaif family is historically connected to the Al Muqrin lineage, which traced its descent to the Banu Hanifa tribes. The family resided in the citadel of Turaif in Diriyah, near modern-day Riyadh. Muhammad bin Saud Al Muqrin, born in Diriyah in 1687, became the local emir of Diriyah in 1727 and is considered the founder of the First Saudi State and the Saud dynasty, named after his father Saud bin Muhammad Al Muqrin. The Al Turaif citadel served as the family residence and power base from which Muhammad bin Saud ruled his emirate until his death in 1765. The family's significance is tied to their role in establishing the First Saudi State through Muhammad bin Saud's alliance with Muhammad bin Abdul Wahhab in 1744-1745, which formed the foundation of modern Saudi Arabia. By the time of Muhammad bin Saud's death, the majority of Najdi people and all those living in southern Najd had become Wahhabi adherents under his rule.
data.description: The Al Turaif family resided in the citadel of Turaif in Diriyah and are connected to the Al Muqrin lineage that founded the First Saudi State. They trace their descent to the Banu Hanifa tribes and served as the ruling family of Diriyah from 1727-1765.
data.originStory: The Al Turaif family traced its descent to the Banu Hanifa tribes, though Muhammad bin Saud was neither a nomadic Bedouin nor a tribal leader, but rather the ruler of the town of Diriyah who had lands there and was involved in financing commercial journeys of merchants.
data.nameEtymology: Al Turaif is named after the citadel of Turaif in Diriyah where the family resided

data.history: Al Mandaq is a city and governorate in Al-Baha Province of Saudi Arabia. It serves as one of the administrative divisions of the province and includes several towns and villages. The city is located in the southwestern region of Saudi Arabia, which is part of the mountainous Asir province area that has historically been distinct from the central Najd region. The research provided does not contain specific information about the Al Mandaq family's origins, migration patterns, or historical development beyond its current administrative status.
data.description: Al Mandaq is a city and governorate in Al-Baha Province, Saudi Arabia, serving as an administrative division that encompasses several towns and villages.
data.modernStatus: Administrative governorate in Al-Baha Province of Saudi Arabia

data.history: The research provided focuses exclusively on the Al Maktoum ruling family of Dubai, not the Al Bawadi family. The extensive documentation covers Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, the Vice President and Prime Minister of the UAE and Ruler of Dubai, and his son Sheikh Hamdan bin Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, Crown Prince of Dubai. The Al Maktoum family traces their lineage to the Bu Falasah branch of the Bani Yas tribal confederation. Sheikh Mohammed was born in 1949 as the third son of Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum, who transformed Dubai into a modern emirate. The family's political prominence began when they became rulers of Dubai, with Sheikh Mohammed assuming power in 2006 following his brother's death. Under his leadership, Dubai has become a global hub for business, tourism, and innovation through projects like the Burj Khalifa, Dubai Metro, and Emirates Airlines. The family maintains strong traditions in equestrian sports, poetry, and governance while modernizing the emirate's infrastructure and economy.
data.description: The research provided does not contain information about the Al Bawadi family, but instead documents the Al Maktoum ruling family of Dubai and their transformation of the emirate into a global city.

data.history: The Al Muhairbi family has held significant political positions in the United Arab Emirates federal government structure. Al Haj bin Abdullah Al Muhairbi served as Speaker of the Federal National Council, the UAE's legislative body, making him one of only nine individuals to hold this prestigious position since the council's inception in 1971. The Federal National Council serves as the main consultative body in the UAE with both legislative and supervisory roles as accorded by the Constitution, consisting of 40 members with advisory powers. The position of Speaker represents one of the highest parliamentary roles in the UAE's federal system, indicating the Al Muhairbi family's integration into the country's political establishment.
data.description: The Al Muhairbi family is a politically prominent Emirati family, notably represented by Al Haj bin Abdullah Al Muhairbi who served as Speaker of the Federal National Council.

data.history: Al-Ahbabi is an Arab Bedouin tribe native to the United Arab Emirates that historically frequented the territory of Abu Dhabi. In the early 20th century, approximately 60 families from the tribe settled permanently in the foothills of Jabal Hafit, where they acknowledged the authority of the Ruler of Abu Dhabi. This settlement marked their transition from nomadic to sedentary life in the region. According to the 1968 census, 319 members of the Al-Ahbabi tribe were recorded, indicating modest population growth since their permanent settlement. The tribe has produced notable figures in modern UAE society, including athletes and government officials, reflecting their integration into contemporary Emirati institutions.

data.history: Al Khouri (Arabic: خوري or Ḫūrī), also transliterated as Khoury, is a surname found among Arab Christians in the Middle East, particularly in the Levant countries of Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, and Palestine. The term derives from the Levantine Arabic word for 'priest,' originating either from the Latin word 'curia' or the French 'curé' meaning parish priest. Historically, the surname was often given to new priests or ministers in the various Eastern churches, replacing their previous surnames, and was passed down to their descendants. The Eastern churches such as the Greek Orthodox Church and Maronite Church allowed married men to become priests, making it common for families to retain the Khoury surname for generations past the original priest's lifetime. The name has numerous spelling variations including El Khouri, El Khoury, Kouri, Coury, and many others, with variations found across different regions where Arab Christians migrated, including Latin America where it appears as Cure, Correa, Juri, and other forms.
data.description: Al Khouri is a surname of Levantine Arab Christian origin meaning 'priest,' historically given to clergy and their descendants in Eastern Christian churches.
data.originStory: The surname originated from the practice of giving the title 'khoury' (priest) as a family name to new priests or ministers in Eastern Christian churches, which was then passed down to their children and descendants through generations.
data.modernStatus: Members of Al Khouri families have achieved prominence across various fields globally, including notable figures like Rami George Khouri (Palestinian-Jordanian American journalist), Lebanese politicians such as those connected to the Khouri political family, and numerous professionals in business, academia, and the arts across the Middle East, Americas, and other regions where Arab Christians have settled.
data.nameEtymology: The name Khoury derives from Levantine Arabic meaning 'priest,' ultimately originating from either the Latin word 'curia' or the French 'curé' meaning parish priest, from Latin 'curatus' meaning 'one responsible for the care (of souls).'

data.history: The Al Zaid family name derives from the Arabic name Zayd/Zaid, which has deep historical roots in Islamic tradition. The most prominent historical figure bearing this name was Zayd ibn Harithah al-Kalbi (c. 581–629 CE), a companion of Prophet Muhammad who was among the first converts to Islam. Born into the Udhra branch of the Kalb tribe in the Najd region of central Arabia, Zayd was captured as a child, sold into slavery, and eventually became Muhammad's adopted son before the practice was abolished in Islam. He served as a military commander and died at the Battle of Mu'tah in 629 CE. In the modern Gulf context, the name Al Zaid gained significant prominence through Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan (1918-2004), the founding father and first president of the United Arab Emirates. Born at Qasr Al Hosn in Abu Dhabi, he was named after his grandfather Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great'), who ruled Abu Dhabi from 1855 to 1909. Sheikh Zayed became ruler of Abu Dhabi in 1966 after replacing his brother in a bloodless coup, and later served as UAE president from 1971 until his death in 2004. The research also indicates that various families bearing the Zaid/Zaidi surname can be found across the Arabian Peninsula, with some tracing lineage to early Islamic figures, though specific genealogical connections to the Al Zaid family discussed here are not established in the available sources.
data.description: Al Zaid refers to families bearing a name derived from the Arabic Zayd, most notably associated with Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founding father of the UAE and ruler of Abu Dhabi from 1966-2004.
data.originStory: The name traces to Arabic origins, with the most historically significant bearer being Zayd ibn Harithah, an early Islamic figure and companion of Prophet Muhammad from the Kalb tribe of central Arabia.
data.modernStatus: In the UAE, the Al Nahyan branch of Al Zaid represents the ruling family of Abu Dhabi and the UAE federation, controlling significant oil wealth and serving as heads of state since 1971.
data.nameEtymology: Derived from the Arabic name Zayd/Zaid, meaning 'growth' or 'abundance', historically associated with Zayd ibn Harithah, companion of Prophet Muhammad

data.history: The House of Al Falahi (Āl Bū Falāḥ) is a tribal Emirati clan that belongs to the Bani Yas tribal confederation. According to J.G. Lorimer, the Al Bu Falah historically constituted a small subsection of the Bani Yas, numbering approximately 15 to 20 households. The group, also known as the Al Nahyan, has traditionally supplied the paramount shaykh of the Bani Yas, who served as the ruler of Abu Dhabi. The Bani Yas came to Abu Dhabi in the 18th century from Liwa Oasis, and the Al Bu Falah have ruled Abu Dhabi since 1793, having previously ruled Liwa. The Al Bu Falah owned date gardens and residences in seven villages in the Liwa region, though most members did not reside there permanently. During winter months, they lived in the desert with their camels, while in summer many participated in pearling expeditions aboard vessels owned by other sections of the Bani Yas. The Al Bu Falah were the first among the Bani Yas to acquire property in the Buraimi Oasis, a practice systematically continued by members of the ruling family. Much of the land formerly associated with the Al Bu Falah village of Muwaiji is now held by the heirs of Muhammad bin Khalifah, with additional holdings belonging to Shaikha Latifah bint Zayid and other ruling family members.
data.description: The House of Al Falahi is a tribal Emirati clan belonging to the Bani Yas confederation, from which the ruling Al Nahyan family of Abu Dhabi descends. They have ruled Abu Dhabi since 1793 and are related to the Al Maktoum ruling family of Dubai.
data.originStory: The House of Al Falahi is a branch of the Bani Yas tribe that came to Abu Dhabi in the 18th century from Liwa Oasis. They were historically a small subsection numbering approximately 15 to 20 households according to historical records.
data.modernStatus: The Al Nahyan family, descended from Al Falahi, currently rules Abu Dhabi and provides the President of the United Arab Emirates. They control multiple sovereign wealth funds including the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority and Mubadala Investment Company with an estimated US$1 trillion in assets under management. In 2024, the family was ranked second in the list of world's richest families.

data.history: The Al Duaij family appears in the research primarily through connections to Bahraini and Kuwaiti royal families. The name appears most prominently in Bahraini royal genealogy, where Sheikha Thajba bint Salman bin Duaij Al Khalifa was the fourth wife of Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa (1872–1942), ruler of Bahrain. Her father, Shaikh Salman bin Duaij, was noted as Shaikh Isa bin Ali's cousin who was killed in Dhahran by the Al Murrah tribe in December 1900. The family also appears in modern contexts through Mohammad Al Duaij, a Kuwaiti businessman born in 1980 who became CEO of Alea Global Group in 2008, and through marriage connections to Kuwaiti royalty, as Mohammad Sabah Al-Salem Al-Sabah is married to Feryal Duaij Al Salman Al Sabah. Additionally, Sheikh Khalifa bin Duaij Al Khalifa has led the Court of the Crown Prince of Bahrain since 2005, and Sheikha Hala bint Duaij Al Khalifa was the former wife of Crown Prince Salman bin Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa until 2005.
data.description: The Al Duaij family appears connected to both Bahraini and Kuwaiti royal families, with members holding significant positions in business and government across the Gulf states.

data.history: The Hayat family encompasses multiple distinct lineages across different regions with no confirmed genealogical connection between them. The most prominent branch is the Sherpao family of Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province, descended from Khan Bahadar Ghulam Haider Khan Sherpao, a Pakistan Movement activist. This Muhammadzai tribal family gained significant political influence through Hayat Mohammad Khan Sherpao (1937-1975), who co-founded the Pakistan People's Party with Zulfiqar Ali Bhutto in 1967 and became the youngest provincial governor in Pakistani history at age 34. His assassination in 1975 at Peshawar University marked a turning point in regional politics, with his legacy continued by his brother Aftab Ahmad Khan Sherpao. In Kuwait, the Hayat name appears among the Persian ('Ajam) community, exemplified by luxury footwear designer Najeeba Hayat (born 1991), who represents the Iranian-descended Kuwaiti families that have maintained cultural and business influence since the 18th century. These various Hayat lineages reflect the diverse ethnic and regional origins found throughout the broader Middle East and South Asia, with no established historical connection between the Pakistani Sherpao political dynasty and other Hayat families.
data.description: Multiple distinct family lineages bearing the Hayat name, most notably the Sherpao political dynasty of Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and Persian-origin families in Kuwait, with no confirmed genealogical connections between branches.
data.originStory: The Pakistani Sherpao branch originates from Khan Bahadar Ghulam Haider Khan Sherpao, a Muhammadzai tribal member and Pakistan Movement activist. The Kuwaiti branch represents families of Iranian descent who settled in Kuwait as part of the broader Persian merchant community migration.
data.modernStatus: The Pakistani branch maintains political influence through the Qaumi Watan Party founded by Aftab Ahmad Khan Sherpao. The Kuwaiti branch includes business figures like luxury designer Najeeba Hayat, representing the Persian-Kuwaiti entrepreneurial community.

data.history: The Tabtabaei family appears prominently in Kuwaiti political records from the late 20th and early 21st centuries, particularly through their participation in the Kuwait National Assembly. Dr. Walid Al-Tabtabaei emerged as a significant political figure, serving as a Member of Parliament and actively engaging in parliamentary proceedings including ministerial grillings. He participated in multiple grilling sessions against various ministers between 1998-2009, including Information Minister Sheikh Saud Al-Nasser Al-Sabah in 1998, Information Minister Mohammed Abulhassan in 2005, Health Minister Sheikh Ahmad Abdullah Al-Ahmad in 2007, Health Minister Maasuma Al-Mubarak in 2007, Awqaf and Islamic Affairs Minister Dr. Abdulla Al-Ma'touq in 2007, and Prime Minister Nasser Al-Mohammed Al-Sabah in 2008-2009. The family name suggests possible connection to the broader Tabatabai scholarly lineage known in Persian and Islamic intellectual traditions, though the specific Kuwaiti branch's origins are not detailed in the available records. The research indicates the family has maintained an active role in Kuwaiti parliamentary opposition politics, particularly during the reform movements of the early 2000s.

data.history: The Al Aradi family name appears in historical records related to Bahrain, with Bahiya Al Aradi being a documented member who became tragically significant during the 2011 Bahraini Uprising. She was killed on March 16, 2011, when she was hit in the back by birdshot pellets fired from a range of 50 to 75 meters during the protests, making her the first woman killed in the uprising. She died at Bahrain Defense Force Hospital from her injuries, with at least one bullet having entered her head from behind with no exit wound. Her death occurred during the wider context of the Arab Spring-inspired protests in Bahrain, where the mainly Shia population was demanding greater political freedom and equality. The family's connection to these significant political events in modern Bahraini history places them within the broader narrative of sectarian tensions and political reform movements that have shaped contemporary Bahrain.
data.description: A Bahraini family known primarily through Bahiya Al Aradi, who became the first woman killed in the 2011 Bahraini Uprising, making her family name historically significant in the context of Bahrain's modern political struggles.
data.modernStatus: The family gained tragic historical significance through Bahiya Al Aradi's death during the 2011 political uprising in Bahrain, with no current business or political activities documented in available records.

data.history: The Al Duwaisan family represents a prominent Kuwaiti diplomatic and political lineage with documented involvement in Kuwait's modern governance and international relations. The most notable contemporary figure is Khaled Abdulaziz Al-Duwaisan (born 1947), who has served as Kuwait's ambassador to the United Kingdom since 1993 and held the prestigious position of Dean of the Diplomatic Corps in London as the longest-serving ambassador. The family has demonstrated significant political engagement in Kuwait's parliamentary system, with Faisal Al-Duwaisan serving as an MP and Chairman of the Human Rights Committee during the 2011-2012 period of political unrest. During the Kuwaiti protests of 2011-2012, Faisal Al-Duwaisan played a notable role in advocating for human rights, specifically requesting the release of online activist Nasser Abul in September 2011. The family's involvement in Kuwait's electoral politics is evidenced by Faisal al-Duwaisan's strong performance in the February 2012 general election, where he received the highest margin of victory with 14,094 votes in his constituency. The Al Duwaisan family has also established connections to international charitable work, with Dalal Al-Duwaisan serving as a patron of Children and Families Across Borders, a UK-based international child protection charity.

data.history: The Al Matrouk family name appears in historical records across the Levant and Gulf regions, with documented presence in multiple locations. In the Palestinian village of Qaqun, the Matrouk clan was among the founding families alongside Abu-Hantash, Zidan, al-Shaykh Ghanem, and al-Hafi clans, residing there until the village's depopulation in 1948. The family is also documented as one of the prominent Kuwaiti families residing in Yarmouk, an upscale area of Kuwait City, alongside other established families like Al-Sabah, Al Abdali, and Al Zamel. In contemporary times, family members have been involved in various incidents across the region, including legal cases in Saudi Arabia and security incidents in the West Bank, suggesting a geographically dispersed family with varied social and professional backgrounds.

data.history: Shah (شاه) is a Persian royal title meaning 'king' that descends from Old Persian xšāyaθiya 'king', derived from the verb xšāy- meaning 'to rule, reign'. The title was used by Persian emperors from the Achaemenid dynasty (6th century BC) through the Pahlavi dynasty until the 1979 Iranian Revolution. The full title Shāhanshāh ('King of Kings') was used by rulers of the Persian Empire, with Mohammad Reza Pahlavi being the last Shah before the monarchy was abolished. The title spread beyond Persia to various Persianate societies including the Ottoman Empire, Mughal Empire, and Afghan dynasties. In the Gulf region, many families bearing the surname Shah are of Iranian descent, particularly among Kuwait's 'Ajam community - Kuwaiti citizens of Iranian origin who historically settled in Kuwait's Sharq district and maintained Persian cultural traditions including language, cuisine, and customs.
data.description: Shah is both a Persian royal title meaning 'king' and a surname commonly found among families of Iranian descent in the Gulf region, particularly Kuwait's 'Ajam community.
data.originStory: The title Shah originates from Old Persian xšāyaθiya meaning 'king', analyzed as deriving from the verb xšāy- 'to rule, reign' with the suffix -iya meaning 'pertaining to reigning, ruling'. It was first used by Achaemenid rulers of the Persian Empire in the 6th century BC.
data.modernStatus: In contemporary Gulf states, particularly Kuwait, families with the surname Shah are often part of the 'Ajam community - Kuwaiti citizens of Iranian descent. Notable modern figures include actress Ratna Pathak Shah and actor Naseeruddin Shah from India, and singer Ema Shah from Kuwait who is described as having a Kuwaiti father of Iranian descent.
data.nameEtymology: Derives from Old Persian xšāyaθiya meaning 'king', from the verb xšāy- 'to rule, reign'. The word is related to Avestan xšaθra- meaning 'power' and 'command', and Sanskrit kṣatra- with the same meaning.

data.history: The Al Muzaini family appears to have connections to multiple regions and contexts within the Arabian Peninsula. According to research on Abdullah al-Qasemi, the controversial Saudi intellectual had family links with the Al-Muzaini family among other Najdi families including Al-Muslim, Al-Husayni and Al-Jamuji. The research indicates these were established kinship networks among prominent families in the Najd region. In the context of tribal genealogy, the Al-Muzaini name appears as a branch (Muzaynah) within the Al-Marawihah section of the larger Harb tribe, one of the major Qahtanite tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. The Harb tribal lands historically extended from the Red Sea coast in Tihamah to the heart of Najd, and from Madinah in the north to Al Qunfudhah in the south. In modern times, members bearing the Al-Muzaini name have achieved prominence in various fields, including academia and business. Dr. Hamza bin Qablan Al-Muzaini has gained recognition as a linguistics professor and translator, particularly for his work translating Noam Chomsky's writings into Arabic, earning the King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz International Award for Translation in 2022. Additionally, the Al Muzaini Exchange Company, established in Kuwait in 1942, represents one of the most successful business ventures associated with this family name, becoming the first exchange company in Kuwait and maintaining significant market presence.
data.description: Al Muzaini is a family name with documented presence across the Arabian Peninsula, appearing both as part of tribal genealogies within the Harb confederation and as prominent families in business and academic circles in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait.
data.originStory: The research suggests multiple potential origins for the Al Muzaini name, including connections to the Harb tribal confederation where Muzaynah appears as a recognized branch, and established kinship networks among Najdi families, though specific founding narratives are not detailed in the available sources.
data.tribalOrigin: Harb tribe (Qahtanite confederation) - specifically mentioned as Muzaynah branch within Al-Marawihah section of Banu Salim
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence in multiple sectors: academic leadership through figures like Dr. Hamza Al-Muzaini at King Saud University and the Literature, Publishing and Translation Commission; significant business presence through Al Muzaini Exchange Company in Kuwait with over 125 branches and 40% market share in exchange services.

data.history: The Al Rayes family's documented history centers around the Lebanese Maronite saint Rafqa Pietra Choboq Ar-Rayès (1832-1914). Born in Himlaya, Matn District, she was the daughter of Saber Mourad El Rayess and Rafqa Gemayel. Her family name appears in the historical record through her father's lineage, though the specific origins or tribal connections of the Al Rayes family are not detailed in the available sources. The family was established in the Mount Lebanon region by at least the early 19th century, as evidenced by Rafqa's birth there in 1832. Her kinsman Joseph Gemayel founded a religious institute for women, indicating the family had connections to influential Lebanese Christian families of the period. The Al Rayes name gained prominence through Saint Rafqa's canonization by Pope John Paul II in 2001, making her a significant figure in Lebanese Maronite history and establishing the family's place in Christian religious heritage.
data.description: A Lebanese Maronite Christian family documented in Mount Lebanon since the early 19th century, most notably connected to Saint Rafqa Pietra Choboq Ar-Rayès (1832-1914), who was canonized in 2001.
data.modernStatus: The family's modern status is primarily associated with religious heritage through Saint Rafqa's canonization and her role as patron saint of lost parents and the sick in the Lebanese Maronite Church.

data.history: The Al Sayed family name appears across multiple regions with distinct lineages and origins. In Palestine/Israel, the al-Sayed family includes individuals like Hisham al-Sayed, born in 1988 in al-Sayyid to parents Manal al-Sayed and Sha'ban al-Sayed. This branch appears to be of Bedouin Arab origin. In Syria, the prominent al-Atassi family claims Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali, with ancestors arriving from Hadhramaut in Yemen during the 15th century after traveling through the Hejaz and Anatolia before settling in Homs. The family's ancestor, Sayyid Ali ibn Khalil al-Attas, was buried in 1508 in what became known as the Atassi Mosque. In India, Sayed Mohiuddin Hussaini Peerzade (known as Sayed Tanveer Hashmi) represents the Hussiani Hashmi Family, claiming descent from Ahl al-Bayt and tracing lineage to the Bijapur Sharif Sufi tradition. In Bahrain, Sayed Mohamed Adnan represents another branch of the family, serving as a professional footballer and national team member. The name variations include al-Sayed, al-Atassi (evolved from al-Attas), and other regional adaptations, indicating multiple distinct family lines rather than a single unified lineage.
data.description: Al Sayed represents multiple distinct family lineages across the Middle East and South Asia, including Palestinian Bedouin Arabs, Syrian families claiming Hashemite descent from Yemen, Indian Sufi families, and Bahraini nationals.
data.originStory: The Syrian al-Atassi branch claims descent from Sayyid Ali ibn Khalil al-Attas of the Ba 'Alawi sada of Hadhramaut in Yemen, who migrated through the Hejaz to Anatolia before settling in Homs, Syria in the 15th century. The Indian Hussiani Hashmi branch traces lineage to Ahl al-Bayt through the Bijapur Sharif Sufi tradition.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains diverse modern roles across regions: political leadership in Syria (multiple presidents including Hashim al-Atassi), religious leadership in India (Sufi orders and Islamic organizations), professional sports in Bahrain, and civilian populations in Palestine/Israel.

folkLegends.title: The Sneezing Saint
folkLegends.story: The name al-Atassi evolved from 'al-Attas' which originated from Sayyid Umar ibn Abdurrahman al-Saqqaf, who was said to have sneezed and praised God while still in his mother's womb, earning him the nickname 'al-Attas' (the sneezer).

data.nameEtymology: Al-Atassi evolved from 'العطاسي' (from 'العطاس,' meaning 'the sneezer') which changed to 'الأطاسي' then to 'الأتاسي', originating from a nickname of Sayyid Umar ibn Abdurrahman al-Saqqaf.

data.history: The Al Mahfoodh family appears to be an Omani family, with Mohammed Mahfoodh Al Ardhi being born in Sur, Oman in May 1961 to Omani parents. His father served as head of the regional customs office, indicating a family tradition in government service. The family moved several times during Mohammed's early life due to his father's career demands, relocating to various parts of western Oman before settling in Al Batana and finally Muscat. During the 1960s, the family faced significant hardships during the Dhofar Rebellion (1965-1975), with their homes in Alaswad being bombed by revolutionaries in 1968 and 1969. His father's government office was frequently attacked, and he narrowly escaped death during a bombing incident when he stepped away from his desk. These experiences of conflict and public service shaped the family's commitment to serving Oman, with Mohammed following his father's encouragement to pursue government internships and eventually joining the Royal Air Force of Oman at age 17. The family has since achieved prominence in both military and business sectors, with Mohammed becoming a distinguished Air Vice Marshal and later transitioning to significant roles in banking and investment management.
data.description: The Al Mahfoodh family is an established Omani family with a strong tradition of government and military service, achieving prominence through Mohammed Mahfoodh Al Ardhi's distinguished career in the Royal Air Force of Oman and subsequent leadership roles in major financial institutions across the Gulf.
data.originStory: The family originates from Oman, specifically from Sur, with Mohammed Mahfoodh Al Ardhi being born there in 1961 to Omani parents. His father worked in government service as head of regional customs offices.
data.modernStatus: The family has achieved significant prominence through Mohammed Mahfoodh Al Ardhi, who serves as Executive Chairman of Investcorp (managing approximately $59.7 billion in assets), Chairman of Bank Sohar, and former Chairman of National Bank of Oman. He is also involved in various business ventures including Rimal Investment Projects, Sundus Investments, and Al Ardhi Energy Services.

data.history: The Al Shamlawi family appears to be a Palestinian family with documented presence in the West Bank, particularly in the villages around the Salfit Governorate. According to research, the family has historical land ownership in the area near what is now the Barkan Industrial Park, with Suleiman Shamlawi from the village of Haris claiming that large parts of the industrial plant are built over 215 dunams of land belonging to his family, which was confiscated by the IDF in 1981. The family provided evidence of tax payments to Jordanian authorities up to 1967, proving their ownership of the land. In legal proceedings that extended over decades, Israel's Supreme Court ruled that Suleiman had sufficiently proved his ownership and could register the land with Israeli authorities, though much of it remained confiscated for the industrial development. The family name also appears in contemporary legal contexts, with Al-Shamlawi mentioned as a lawyer and human rights defender who represented opposition figures in Bahrain, though this may represent a different branch or unrelated individuals with the same surname. Additionally, refugee families named Shamlawi are documented as part of the 5% minority population in Az-Zawiya village in the Salfit Governorate.
data.description: A Palestinian family with documented historical land ownership in the West Bank, particularly around the Salfit Governorate area, involved in lengthy legal battles over land confiscated for Israeli settlement development.
data.modernStatus: The family remains engaged in legal proceedings over ancestral lands, with some members working as legal professionals and human rights advocates in the broader Middle East region.

data.history:
The Hassan family referenced in this research encompasses multiple distinct lineages with no confirmed genealogical connections. Hassan Mostafavi Khomeini represents the Iranian clerical Khomeini family, being the grandson of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini and son of Ahmad Khomeini and Fatemeh Tabatabai. Born in 1972, he has served as Custodian of the Mausoleum of Khomeini since 1995 and holds moderate reformist views contrasting with his grandfather's hardline positions. Hassan Rouhani, born Hassan Fereydoun in 1948, represents a separate Iranian political family from Sorkheh near Semnan, with his father Haj Asadollah Fereydoun being a spice shop owner who was politically active against the Shah. Rouhani changed his surname from Fereydoun to Rouhani (meaning 'spiritual' or 'cleric') and served as Iran's seventh president from 2013 to 2021.

Nazia Hassan represents an entirely different lineage from the Pakistani entertainment industry. Born in Karachi in 1965 to businessman Basir Hassan and social worker Muniza Basir, she became a pioneering pop singer who revolutionized South Asian music in the 1980s. Her family had no political connections but were prominent in social and business circles. The research provides no evidence of any genealogical, tribal, or regional connections between these distinct Hassan families from Iran and Pakistan.

The research also references various other Hassan individuals in different contexts - from Bahraini political figures to members of ruling families - but provides insufficient detail to establish comprehensive family histories or connections. Each Hassan lineage appears to represent separate family traditions with distinct origins, careers, and geographical bases across the Gulf and broader Middle Eastern region.
data.description: Multiple distinct Hassan families from Iran and Pakistan with no confirmed genealogical connections, including the Iranian Khomeini clerical family, the Rouhani political family, and the Pakistani Hassan entertainment family.
data.modernStatus: Various Hassan families hold different positions: Hassan Khomeini serves as a religious custodian in Iran, the Rouhani family includes former Iranian president Hassan Rouhani, and the Pakistani Hassan family was prominent in entertainment before Nazia Hassan's death in 2000.
data.nameEtymology: Hassan is a common Arabic name meaning 'handsome' or 'good'; Rouhani changed his surname from Fereydoun to Rouhani meaning 'spiritual' or 'cleric'

migrationPath.description: Hassan Khomeini appointed as custodian following father's death

data.history: The research reveals two distinct Tariq family lineages in different contexts. The first is represented by Tariq Jamil, a Pakistani Islamic cleric born in 1953 in Mian Channu, Punjab, who belongs to a well-off Punjabi Rajput family of large landholders descending from Prithviraj Chauhan, a 12th-century ruler. His family ruled Tulamba during the reign of Sher Shah Suri in the 16th century, who also distributed the lands around Tulamba. The second lineage is the Al Said royal family of Oman, specifically the branch of Tariq bin Taimur Al Said (1921-1980), who was a member of the Omani royal family and the first prime minister of the Cabinet of Oman. Tariq bin Taimur was the son of Sultan Taimur bin Feisal and his third wife, Kamile İlgiray, a Circassian woman. His son, Sultan Haitham bin Tariq, currently rules Oman as Sultan and Prime Minister since January 2020. The Al Said family has ruled Oman for centuries as part of the established royal dynasty.
data.description: Two distinct Tariq lineages: a Pakistani Rajput family descended from medieval rulers, and the Omani Al Said royal family currently ruling Oman.
data.originStory: The Pakistani Tariq family traces its origins to Prithviraj Chauhan, a 12th-century ruler, with the family ruling Tulamba during Sher Shah Suri's 16th-century reign. The Omani Tariq lineage belongs to the Al Said royal family, with Tariq bin Taimur being born to Sultan Taimur bin Feisal and Circassian mother Kamile İlgiray.
data.modernStatus: The Pakistani Tariq branch is represented by Islamic cleric Tariq Jamil, who has established MTJ Brand clothing company and the Maulana Tariq Jamil Foundation. The Omani branch holds supreme power with Sultan Haitham bin Tariq ruling as Sultan and Prime Minister of Oman since 2020.
