data.history: Based on the available research, the Mittal family's documented history begins with Lakshmi Niwas Mittal, born on 15 June 1950 in Sadulpur, Rajasthan to a Marwari Hindu family. His father, Mohanlal Mittal, ran a steel business called Nippon Denro Ispat. In 1976, due to government restrictions on steel production in India, 26-year-old Lakshmi Mittal established his first steel factory, PT Ispat Indo, in Sidoarjo, East Java, Indonesia. This marked the beginning of the family's global steel empire. Through strategic acquisitions in the 1990s and 2000s, including operations in Trinidad and Tobago, Ireland, Kazakhstan, Romania, and Poland, Mittal built what would become ArcelorMittal, the world's second-largest steelmaking company. The family has maintained significant business interests in India, with operations near Mumbai run by Lakshmi's younger brothers Pramod and Vinod Mittal, though Lakshmi has no connection with these Indian operations. His son Aditya Mittal, born 22 January 1976, became CEO of ArcelorMittal in February 2021, continuing the family's leadership in the global steel industry.
data.description: A Marwari Hindu business family from Rajasthan, India, that built a global steel empire through ArcelorMittal, becoming one of the world's most prominent industrial dynasties.
data.originStory: The documented origin shows the family as Marwari Hindus from Sadulpur, Rajasthan, with Mohanlal Mittal operating a steel business called Nippon Denro Ispat before his son Lakshmi expanded internationally.
data.modernStatus: Leading global steel industry through ArcelorMittal, the world's second-largest steelmaking company. Lakshmi Mittal serves as executive chairman while son Aditya Mittal became CEO in 2021. Net worth estimated at £15.444 billion as of 2025.

data.history: The Al Harthy family is documented as one of the prominent tribes in Oman, particularly prevalent in Ibra, which is located about 170 km from Muscat in the Ash Sharqiyah Region. Hamoud bin Abdullah al-Harthi was born around 1940 in al-Mudayrib in the al-Sharqiyah region and belonged to the Hirth tribe. He received his education in Zanzibar Sultanate and studied law in Cairo and Baghdad University, later working in Kuwait and Abu Dhabi before returning to Oman in 1971 for government service. Al-Harthi held several high-ranking positions including chairman of the Consultative Assembly from 1983-1991, Minister of Justice, Awqaf and Islamic Affairs from 1991, and chairman of the Council of State of Oman from 1997 until his death in 2004. The family has maintained significant presence in modern Oman, with connections to business and mountaineering achievements, as evidenced by Nadhira Al Harthy becoming the first Omani woman to climb Mount Everest in 2019, and marriages into prominent business families like the Al Barwanis.
data.description: Al Harthy is a prominent Omani family with documented tribal origins in the Hirth tribe, known for significant political leadership roles and modern achievements in mountaineering and business connections.
data.originStory: The Al Harthy family originates from the Hirth tribe, with documented presence in the al-Sharqiyah region of Oman, particularly in areas like al-Mudayrib and Ibra.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence through political leadership roles in Oman's government structure, achievements in international mountaineering, and connections to major Omani business families through marriage alliances with families like the Al Barwanis.

data.history: Based on the available research, Suhail Bahwan was an 86-year-old Omani conglomerate industry executive who died on November 23, 2025. The research indicates his involvement in the automotive sector through his connection to Suhail Bahwan Automobiles, an Omani company that was allegedly used in a financial scheme involving former Nissan-Renault CEO Carlos Ghosn. According to French authorities' 2022 international warrant, Ghosn allegedly funneled millions of dollars of Renault funds through Suhail Bahwan Automobiles for personal use, including the purchase of a 120-foot yacht. The company appears to have served as an intermediary in complex financial transactions involving major automotive corporations. However, the research provides limited information about the broader Bahwan family history, business empire development, or their origins and establishment in Oman.

data.history: Moosa Abdul Rahman Hassan was an Omani businessman, tribal leader, and landlord born in the old town of Muscat who completed his studies at the American Mission School. In 1927, he established a firm supplying coal and foodstuffs to British ships and frigates in Muscat, becoming one of the pioneer users and establishers of the Post Office Box system in the Sultanate of Oman through his P.O. Box 4 Muscat. Between the 1940s and 1960s, he operated a money transfer service that functioned as the first money transferring and wiring service hub for Omanis living abroad in East Africa, Gulf States, and India, providing this service well before Western Union was established in the region. He became a founding member and local director of The British Bank of The Middle East in 1948 and was later involved in establishing Bank of Oman, Bahrain and Kuwait in partnership with Bank of Bahrain and Kuwait (BBK) in the mid-1970s. His business empire expanded to include representation of British Petroleum Company (BP) with a network of filling stations, partnerships in Gary McKenzie Moosa Towell Group of Companies (later Oman United Agencies), and agencies for various automotive brands including Holden, Bedford Trucks, Pontiac, GMC, and others. He played instrumental governmental roles including serving as Vice Chairman of Oman's Currency Board established in 1972 and being appointed to committees for resolving commercial disputes and forming Oman's Chamber of Commerce and Industry. Sultan Qaboos bin Said granted him Oman's Civil Medal in 1983 for his social, economic, and political contributions to Oman, and he died on 21 April 1987, survived by one daughter (Ruqayyah) and two sons (Abdullah and Ali).
data.description: Moosa Abdul Rahman Hassan was a pioneering Omani businessman and tribal leader who established one of Oman's first major trading enterprises in 1927 and played a crucial role in developing the country's early banking, postal, and commercial infrastructure. He served in key governmental positions and received Oman's Civil Medal for his contributions to the nation's development.
data.modernStatus: The family business interests are currently managed by his surviving children Abdullah and Ali Hassan, who continue to oversee the family's business, social, and governmental interests through the Moosa AbdulRahman Group.

data.history: The Al Milhim family appears to be connected to the Lebanese Shihab dynasty that ruled Mount Lebanon from 1697-1842. Milhim al-Shihab (also spelled Milhem) served as Emir of Mount Lebanon from 1729-1754, succeeding Haydar al-Shihab in 1732. The Shihab family originated as Sunni Muslims, though they later converted to Christianity by the mid-18th century. Milhim repeatedly succeeded in avoiding payment of regular taxes to Ottoman authorities, and in 1748 the governor of Damascus launched a punitive expedition against him. In the 1750s, Milhim attempted to acquire a firman confirming his authority over the Shuf region and his nephew Quasim's authority over Byblos, but the attempt failed when the political climate in Istanbul changed after the death of Sultan Osman III in 1757. After his abdication in 1753, the administration was run by Mansur and Ahmad al-Shihab for several years. The name Milhim also appears among Palestinian political figures, with Mohammed Milhim serving as mayor of Halhul in the West Bank from 1976-2004, and the Milhem surname being noted as an Arabic name meaning 'King of the night.'
data.description: Al Milhim appears to be associated with the Lebanese Shihab dynasty, with Milhim al-Shihab serving as Emir of Mount Lebanon from 1729-1754, and the name also found among Palestinian political figures in the modern era.

data.history: The Al Humaid family mentioned in this research appears to refer specifically to the Al Nuaimi ruling family of Ajman, with 'Al Humaid' likely being a patronymic reference to ancestors named Humaid. The research shows Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III (1902-1981) served as the 9th ruler of Ajman from 1928 until 1981, ruling for 53 years and working to build modern Ajman. He established the Ajman Police in 1967 and was a founder of the United Arab Emirates. His son Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III succeeded him in 1981, becoming the 10th ruler of Ajman after serving as deputy ruler since 1960. The ruling family belongs to the Al Na'im tribe and has maintained continuous rule over Ajman through multiple generations. Sheikh Humaid III received his early education in Dubai in the 1940s and 1950s, then studied in Cairo, becoming active in state affairs in the early 1970s when Ajman joined the UAE in December 1971.
data.description: The Al Humaid family refers to the Al Nuaimi ruling dynasty of Ajman, belonging to the Al Na'im tribe, who have governed the emirate since at least 1928.
data.modernStatus: Currently rules the Emirate of Ajman as part of the UAE, with Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III as the current ruler since 1981 and his son Sheikh Ammar bin Humaid Al Nuaimi as Crown Prince and President of the executive board.

data.history: The Al Salim family represents a prominent branch of the Al-Sabah ruling family of Kuwait, tracing their lineage to Salim Al-Mubarak Al-Sabah (1864-1921), who served as the ninth ruler of Kuwait from 1917 to 1921. Salim Al-Mubarak was the second son of Mubarak I and established what became known as the Al-Salim branch of the ruling dynasty. His rule lasted from February 5, 1917, until his death on February 23, 1921, after succeeding his brother Jabir II. Before becoming ruler, Salim served as governor of Kuwait City between 1915 and 1917 and was chief cavalry and infantry commander during the 1920 Battle of Jahra. The Al-Salim branch produced several significant leaders in Kuwait's modern history, including Sheikh Sabah Al-Salim Al-Sabah (1913-1977), who served as the 12th Emir of Kuwait from 1965 to 1977, and Sheikh Saad Al-Abdullah Al-Salim Al-Sabah (1930-2008), who briefly served as Emir in 2006. The family has maintained its prominence through various governmental positions, with members serving in key roles including Prime Minister, Crown Prince, and other high-ranking positions in Kuwait's political structure.
data.description: The Al-Salim family is a branch of Kuwait's ruling Al-Sabah dynasty, founded by Salim Al-Mubarak Al-Sabah who ruled Kuwait from 1917-1921. The family has produced multiple Emirs and high-ranking government officials throughout Kuwait's modern history.
data.modernStatus: The Al-Salim branch continues to hold significant positions within Kuwait's government structure, with family members serving in various governmental roles and maintaining their status as part of the ruling Al-Sabah dynasty.

data.history: The Al Hameli (الهاملي) are documented as one of the branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, also known as Hawamil (الهوامل). According to historical sources, the Hawamil were reported to have originally been part of the Al Ali tribe before their incorporation into the Bani Yas confederation. The Bani Yas, including the Al Hameli branch, originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia and settled in the Liwa Oasis around 1700 in present-day Abu Dhabi territory. The confederation later expanded their settlements to include areas around Al Ain, Buraimi, and various coastal settlements. As part of the larger Bani Yas confederation, the Al Hameli participated in the historical migrations and political developments that shaped the region, including the 1793 migration to Abu Dhabi island and subsequent establishment of settlements throughout what would become the UAE. The family name appears in historical records as one of the recognized sections of the Bani Yas, with contemporary sources identifying more than 20 sections as forming part of the confederation.
data.description: The Al Hameli (Hawamil) are a branch of the Bani Yas tribal confederation with origins in Najd, who settled in the Liwa Oasis around 1700 and were historically part of the Al Ali tribe before joining the Bani Yas. They are documented as one of the constituent sections of this influential tribal confederation in the UAE.
data.originStory: The Al Hameli, known historically as the Hawamil, were originally part of the Al Ali tribe before their incorporation into the Bani Yas confederation. The Bani Yas confederation, including the Hawamil section, descended from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a and originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia before settling in the Liwa Oasis around 1700.

data.history: The Al Mannaei (Arabic: المناعي) tribe originated from Banu Tamim and is based primarily in the Arab states of the Persian Gulf. In Bahrain, their traditional home has been Galali village, where they became known for involvement in the natural pearl trade. The history of Al-Mannai family trading dates back to 1824, when Salem Bin Darwish Al Mannai, the head of the family, established the pearl trading business in Bahrain. Al Mannai tribes are located throughout the Persian Gulf area, especially in Qatar and Bahrain. In Qatar, the Al Mannai were historically based in the village of Abu Dhalouf, where J. G. Lorimer noted in 1908 that the village was inhabited by 70 families of the Al Mannai tribe. By 1939, the British Political Resident remarked that Emir Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani's first wife was from the tribe, and that the Qatar section had 70 to 80 fighting men and was led by Nasser bin Abdullah Al Attiyah. Today, this demonstrates the establishment of one of the largest corporations in Qatar, the Mannai Corporation, which is involved in several industries with subsidiary entities extended worldwide and bases in international locations.
data.description: The Al Mannaei is an Arab tribe originating from Banu Tamim, primarily based in the Persian Gulf states of Bahrain and Qatar. They have been historically involved in pearl trading since 1824 and have established the major Mannai Corporation in Qatar.
data.originStory: The Al Mannai tribe originated from Banu Tamim, an Arab tribe, and settled primarily in the Persian Gulf region with their traditional home in Galali village, Bahrain.
data.modernStatus: The family has established the Mannai Corporation, one of the largest corporations in Qatar involved in several industries with subsidiary entities extended worldwide and bases in international locations.

data.history: The Marar (singular Al Marri) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates, forming a subsection of the Bani Yas tribal confederation. They are traditionally Hinawi, Maliki Sunnis who became part of the tribal confederation brought together by Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, 'Zayed the Great', of Abu Dhabi. In 1890, under Zayed's leadership, the Marar and Manasir tribes were involved in a series of raids on the coast between Abu Dhabi and Dubai, capturing 400 camels and 100 horses. The Marar had also settled in Dubai, but in 1891, some 400 men of the Marar, considering themselves ill-treated by Sheikh Rashid bin Maktoum of Dubai, ended the pearling season by sailing their boats to Sharjah and subsequently settling there. By the turn of the 19th century, the Marar were distributed across multiple emirates: 200 houses in the Layyah area of Sharjah, 40 in Abu Dhabi, and 30 in Dubai. The Bedouin Marar consisted of some 70 families in Liwa, closely related to the Mazari, while the Marar of the towns were traditionally pearl divers. The area of Deira in Dubai that was traditionally settled by the Marar today bears their name, transliterated variously as Al Murar and also commonly Al Marar.
data.description: The Marar is a subsection of the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the UAE, traditionally pearl divers and Bedouins who settled across Abu Dhabi, Dubai, and Sharjah. They are known for their 1891 migration from Dubai to Sharjah due to disputes with the ruling sheikh.
data.modernStatus: The Marar maintain their historical settlements in various emirates, with the Deira area of Dubai still bearing their name (Al Marar/Al Murar). They have notable connections to ruling families through marriage alliances.

data.history: Based on the research provided, the Al Otaishan family appears to have historical connections to the Al-Qassim region of Saudi Arabia. The most significant documented figure is Turki bin Abdullah Al Otaishan, who served as the Saudi Emir of Ras Tanura and led a notable military expedition in 1952. On 31 August 1952, Turki bin Abdullah Al Otaishan led approximately 80 Saudi Arabian guards, 40 of whom were armed, in occupying Hamasa village in the Buraimi Oasis as part of the broader Buraimi dispute between Saudi Arabia and the Trucial States. This action was part of Saudi Arabia's territorial claims over oil-rich areas in what is now the UAE. The research also indicates connections to Prince Mutaib bin Abdullah Al Saud, whose mother was Munira bint Mohammed bin Abdullah Al Otaishan, suggesting the family maintained significant status and connections to the Saudi royal family through marriage alliances. The family appears to be part of the broader network of notable families from the Al-Qassim region, which has historically been a key support base for the Al Saud family and a center of influence in the Arabian Peninsula.
data.description: A Saudi Arabian family with documented historical connections to the Al-Qassim region and notable involvement in mid-20th century territorial disputes in the Arabian Gulf.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains connections to the Saudi royal family through marriage, with documented ties to senior princes including Prince Mutaib bin Abdullah Al Saud.

data.history: The Al Juhani family derives from the Juhaynah tribe, which historically lived around the city of Medina in Arabia and continues to inhabit that region today. The Juhaynah tribe has ancient roots in the Arabian Peninsula, with historical figures like Ma'bad ibn Khalid al-Juhani (died 699 CE) documented as early members who lived around Medina during the early Islamic period. The family name has been associated with both religious scholarship and controversy throughout history, with Ma'bad al-Juhani being noted as an early Islamic theological figure who was executed for his religious views by Caliph Abd al-Malik in Damascus. In modern times, the Al Juhani name gained international attention through Khalid Mohammad bin Muslim Al-Arawi Al-Juhani (1975-2003), a Saudi national who became associated with extremist activities and was identified as one of the perpetrators in the 2003 Riyadh compound bombings. The family name thus represents the complex history of Arabian tribal lineages, spanning from early Islamic scholarship to modern controversies.
data.description: The Al Juhani family originates from the Juhaynah tribe that has historically inhabited the area around Medina, Saudi Arabia, with documented members spanning from early Islamic times to the present day.
data.originStory: The Al Juhani family traces its lineage to the Juhaynah tribe, which has been established around the city of Medina in Arabia since ancient times and continues to inhabit that region today.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary members of the Al Juhani family continue to live in Saudi Arabia, though the family gained notoriety in the early 21st century due to association with extremist activities.

data.history: The Al Murrah (Arabic: آل مرة) are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe, comprising approximately 15,000 individuals organized into seven clans within the Saudi branch. Historically operating as camel-herding nomads, they controlled and travelled through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula, covering up to 3,000 kilometres annually. The tribe has maintained a significant presence across multiple Gulf states, including Qatar, Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Bahrain. In Qatar specifically, the Al Murrah historically constituted a large proportion of the ethnic population and played crucial roles in the country's political landscape. However, their relationship with Qatari leadership became strained following their support for Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani, who was deposed in 1995. In 1996, 119 Al Murrah members attempted to overthrow the new Emir Sheikh Hamad Al Thani, resulting in death sentences for 19 members and life imprisonment for 33 others, though no executions were carried out. The tribe's political exclusion continued under current leadership, with Qatar revoking citizenship of the sheikh and 54 tribespeople in 2017, and barring Al Murrah members from participating in the 2021 Qatari general election, which led to protests.
data.description: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from Banu Yam, historically nomadic camel herders who controlled vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula. They maintain significant populations across Gulf states but face political restrictions in Qatar due to their opposition to the current ruling family.
data.originStory: The Al Murrah are descended from the Banu Yam tribe, representing one of the traditional Bedouin tribal lineages of the Arabian Peninsula.
data.modernStatus: The tribe comprises approximately 15,000 individuals organized into seven clans in Saudi Arabia, with significant populations across Gulf states. In Qatar, they face political restrictions including citizenship revocation and electoral exclusion due to their historical opposition to the current Al Thani ruling family. They maintain friendly relations with UAE and Saudi Arabia while opposing the current Iranian regime.

data.history: The Al Bloushi (البلوشي) family surname is an Arabized form of the term Balochi or Baluchi, typically denoting Baloch ancestry from Balochistan. Many people carrying this surname trace their ancestral origins to Balochistan — a region in southwestern Pakistan and southeastern Iran — with their ancestors primarily coming from the Makran coast in the 18th century. The Al-Balushi surname is common across Gulf countries including Oman, the UAE, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Eastern Saudi Arabia and Southern Iraq, where the majority now speak Arabic while some retain their native Balochi language and are mainly Sunni Muslims. The family has produced notable figures across multiple countries, particularly in sports, with Qatari footballer Talal Al-Bloushi having a distinguished career with Al Sadd and the Qatar national team, and other family members achieving prominence in football across Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, and the UAE. The historical connection between the Baloch homeland and the Gulf was strengthened through territories like Gwadar Port, which served as a crucial link between Arabia, South Asia, and colonial European powers, and was under Omani rule for nearly two centuries until 1958.
data.description: Al Bloushi is a Gulf Arab family name of Baloch origin, with ancestors who migrated from the Makran coast of Balochistan in the 18th century. The family is now established across multiple Gulf states, particularly known for producing athletes and maintaining both Arabic and Balochi cultural heritage.
data.originStory: The Al Bloushi family traces its origins to Balochistan, a region spanning southwestern Pakistan and southeastern Iran. Their ancestors primarily migrated from the Makran coast during the 18th century, establishing themselves across the Gulf states while maintaining connections to their Baloch heritage.
data.modernStatus: The family has achieved notable success in sports across Gulf countries, with family members becoming prominent footballers in Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, and the UAE. They maintain presence across multiple Gulf states in various professional fields.

data.history: The Al Rawi family traces its origins to the city of Rawah (also spelled Rawa) in the Al Anbar Governorate of western Iraq. The family name derives from this ancestral city, indicating their geographic roots in the Anbar region. Members of the Al Rawi family have dispersed from Iraq due to various circumstances, including political instability and conflict. Ahmed Hisham Ali Musa Al-Rawi's family moved from Baghdad to Syria in 2006, then to Qatar in 2007, representing the migration patterns of many Iraqi families during periods of regional turmoil. The family has produced notable figures in literature, sports, and academia, with members achieving international recognition in their respective fields while maintaining connections to their Iraqi heritage.
data.description: An Iraqi family originally from Rawah District in Al Anbar Governorate, with members dispersed across the Middle East who have achieved prominence in literature, sports, and academia.
data.originStory: The Al Rawi family originates from the city of Rawah in the Al Anbar Governorate of western Iraq, with the family name directly reflecting this geographic origin.
data.modernStatus: The family has members across multiple countries, with notable achievements in international football (Ahmed Al-Rawi representing Qatar national team), literature (Shahad Al Rawi as an acclaimed novelist), and various professional fields.
data.nameEtymology: The surname Al-Rawi derives from the city of Rawah (Rawa) in Al Anbar Governorate, Iraq, indicating the family's ancestral connection to this western Iraqi city.

data.history: The Al Sulayti (also spelled Al Sulaiti) family has documented presence in the Gulf region spanning multiple generations, with evidence of their involvement in Qatar's cultural and theatrical development since the mid-20th century. Saleh Mohammed Al Sulaiti was active in Qatar's early theatre scene in the 1950s-1960s, writing politically themed plays including 'Between Past and Present' which depicted the Israeli-Palestinian conflict and prompted government reaction in 1961. The family's most prominent modern figure is Ghanem Al Sulaiti, born in 1956 in Manama, Bahrain, who represents the family's mixed Gulf heritage - his father being Qatari, mother Emirati, and grandmother Saudi. Ghanem Al Sulaiti became a pioneering figure in Qatari theatre, founding Qatar's first theatrical production company in 1986 and writing numerous acclaimed plays including 'The Fighters' (1985) which won international recognition at the Carthage Theatre Festival in Tunisia. His works often address Arab political and social themes, with his 2018 television series 'What's Happening?' directly satirizing the 2017 Gulf blockade of Qatar.
data.description: A Gulf family with documented theatrical and cultural contributions to Qatar, known for their involvement in the country's dramatic arts development and political commentary through theatre.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains cultural prominence through Ghanem Al Sulaiti's continued work as Qatar's leading playwright and theatre producer, with his company producing internationally recognized works and his plays being performed throughout the Gulf region.

data.history: Based on the available research, Al Shabib appears to be connected to the Omani royal family Al Said through Shabib bin Taimur Al Said, who was a son of Sultan Taimur bin Feisal and brother to Sultan Said bin Taimur. Shabib was part of the Omani royal lineage during the early-to-mid 20th century, though specific details about his life and role are not provided in the research. The name Shabib itself has ancient Arabic origins, with historical figures bearing this name dating back to the 7th century, including notable personalities who participated in early Islamic events such as the Battle of Karbala in 680 CE. However, the specific origins, migration patterns, and detailed history of the Al Shabib family as a distinct entity are not documented in the available research materials.

data.history: The Al Hamoud family emerged as the ruling dynasty of Al Buraimi oasis in northern Oman, with their leadership documented from around 1290 AH (approximately 1873 CE). The family belonged to the Al Nuaimi tribal confederation, specifically identified as Al Hamoud Al Qurtasi Al Nuaimi. Their rule began when Sheikh Ali bin Hamoud Al Nuaimi assumed guardianship of the children of Sheikh Al Filani Al Qassar, the ruler of Dhank, and through political maneuvering seized control of Al Buraimi. His son, Mohammed bin Ali bin Hamoud, succeeded him in leadership and sought to make Al Buraimi the capital of the region. Mohammed bin Ali had strong ties with the Bani Jaber tribe in Sa'ara village and eventually assumed leadership of the oasis around 1290 AH. The family constructed significant fortifications including Al-Hillah Fort, built by Sheikh Mohammed bin Ali bin Hamoud Al-Nuaimi, which served as their administrative center and replaced the earlier Al Khandaq Fort. The Al Hamoud family's rule persisted through multiple generations until 1950, with their final ruler being Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan bin Mohammed Al Hamoud Al Qurtasi Al Nuaimi, who oversaw the rebuilding of Al Buraimi. Their governance ended with the conclusion of the Buraimi Crisis, a border conflict involving Saudi Arabia, Oman, and the United Arab Emirates.
data.description: The Al Hamoud were the ruling family of Al Buraimi oasis from approximately 1873 to 1950, belonging to the Al Nuaimi tribal confederation. They established political control through strategic alliances and constructed major fortifications that served as their power base.
data.originStory: Sheikh Ali bin Hamoud Al Nuaimi gained control of Al Buraimi by assuming guardianship of the children of Sheikh Al Filani Al Qassar, the ruler of Dhank, and cleverly seized political control of the oasis through this position of trust.
data.modernStatus: The Al Hamoud family's political rule ended in 1950 with the conclusion of the Buraimi Crisis, and no current political or business activities are documented in the research.

data.history:
The Al Bu Falah (also known as the House of Al Falahi) is a tribal Emirati clan that belongs to the Bani Yas tribal confederation, from which the ruling Al Nahyan family of Abu Dhabi descends. According to historical sources, the Bani Yas tribe originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia and settled in the Liwa Oasis around 1700. The Al Bu Falah constituted a small subsection of the Bani Yas, numbering approximately 15 to 20 households according to J. G. Lorimer, and traditionally supplied the paramount shaykh of the Bani Yas confederation.

The Al Bu Falah were the first among the Bani Yas to acquire property in the Buraimi Oasis, establishing a pattern of land acquisition that continued under the ruling family. During winter months, they lived in the desert with their camels, while in summer many participated in pearling expeditions. The founding of the Al Bu Falah dynasty as rulers is attributed to Sheikh Dhiyab ibn Isa Al Nahyan, who led the Bani Yas from 1761 to 1793. In 1793, following the discovery of fresh water, Dhiyab ordered his son Shakbut to move to the island of Abu Dhabi, where they built a village and fort that would become the capital of the emirate.

The Al Bu Falah have ruled Abu Dhabi continuously since 1793, though the early period was marked by instability with five rulers overthrown and eight killed in coups between 1793 and 1966. The family's fortunes transformed with the discovery of oil in 1958 at Umm Shaif and later onshore discoveries. Under Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan's leadership from 1966 to 2004, oil revenues funded massive development programs. Today, the Al Nahyan family controls sovereign wealth funds including the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority and Mubadala Investment Company with an estimated US$1 trillion in assets, ranking them second among the world's richest families in 2024.
data.description: The Al Bu Falah is a branch of the Bani Yas tribal confederation that has ruled Abu Dhabi since 1793. The ruling Al Nahyan family descends from this clan, which originated in Najd and settled in the Liwa Oasis around 1700.
data.originStory: The Al Bu Falah trace their founding as rulers to Sheikh Dhiyab ibn Isa Al Nahyan, who led a hunting party from Liwa in 1761 that tracked a gazelle to a brackish spring on an island. The gazelle became the symbol of Abu Dhabi and gave it its name (literally 'Father of the Gazelle'). In 1793, Dhiyab ordered his son Shakbut to move to the island, where they built a village of some 20 houses and a fort, establishing Abu Dhabi as their seat of power.
data.modernStatus: The Al Bu Falah, through the Al Nahyan ruling family, controls the Emirate of Abu Dhabi and provides the President of the United Arab Emirates. They manage sovereign wealth funds with approximately US$1 trillion in assets and were ranked as the world's second richest family in 2024.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Muharib family name appears in connection with Mohammed Abdulaziz Mohammed Al-Muharib, who was executed by Saudi Arabia in the 2016 mass execution. The research shows that this individual was among 47 people executed on January 2, 2016, convicted of terrorism-related charges. However, the research provides no information about the broader Al Muharib family history, their origins, tribal connections, or how they came to their current location. The connection between this individual and any larger Al Muharib family structure, business activities, or political involvement remains undocumented in the available sources. Without additional research specifically focused on the Al Muharib family genealogy and history, no comprehensive narrative about their origins, migration patterns, or historical development can be established.

data.history:
The Al Manasir is a major Bedouin tribe of the United Arab Emirates, traditionally one of the most important tribes in the emirate of Abu Dhabi alongside the Bani Yas. Historically nomadic, warlike and fiercely independent, the Manasir roamed between Buraimi and Qatar, from the Persian Gulf coast to Liwa, subsisting through date farming, pearling, camel breeding, and operating camel trains for trade. Unlike their settled counterparts the Bani Yas, the Manasir did not necessarily consider themselves dependents of the Sheikh of Abu Dhabi, maintaining their essential independence with their own sheikhs while cooperating closely with Abu Dhabi's rulers.

The tribe was traditionally predominant in the desert areas between Buraimi and Qatar and the Persian Gulf coast to Liwa. Some 1,400 Manasir roamed this tribal area at the turn of the 20th century, growing to approximately 4,000 by the 1950s, with 2,800 living in Abu Dhabi emirate and the balance in other Trucial States. The Manasir were exempted from paying the customary date tax to the Ruler of Abu Dhabi. Formerly subject to the Wahhabi government, they had asserted their independence by the turn of the 20th century and were closer to Abu Dhabi.

As revenues from the pearl trade declined in the late 1920s, the Manasir's traditional economy suffered, leading to increased raiding on Northern emirates communities. The tribe adapted by working for oil companies like ARAMCO and Qatar Petroleum, though they typically worked only until immediate economic needs were met. By the 1950s, the Ruler of Abu Dhabi had 85 regularly paid Manasir retainers, recognizing the tribe as a potent and influential force.
data.description: The Al Manasir is a historically nomadic Bedouin tribe of the UAE, traditionally one of the most important tribes in Abu Dhabi emirate alongside the Bani Yas. Known for their fierce independence and warrior traditions, they roamed between Buraimi and Qatar, maintaining close cooperation with Abu Dhabi's rulers while preserving their autonomy under their own sheikhs.
data.modernStatus: The Al Manasir have produced several notable modern figures including Mariam Al Mansouri (UAE's first female fighter pilot), Hazza Al Mansouri (first Emirati astronaut in space), and Sultan bin Saeed Al Mansoori (former UAE Minister of Economy 2008-2020). Members also hold prominent positions in other Gulf states including Qatar and Bahrain.

data.history: The research provides information about multiple distinct Al Mansour families across the Gulf region, with no evidence of shared lineage. The most prominent is the Al Nahyan branch in the UAE, where Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan (born 1970) serves as vice president and deputy prime minister of the UAE, as well as minister of presidential court. He is the son of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan and Sheikha Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi, belonging to the ruling family of Abu Dhabi. His mother's lineage is known as 'Bani Fatima' or sons of Fatima, with five full brothers including the current UAE President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. In Saudi Arabia, a separate Al Saud branch existed with Mansour bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1921-1951), who served as Saudi Arabia's first defense minister from 1943-1951. His mother was Lebanese woman Shahida, reportedly King Abdulaziz's favorite wife. In Kuwait, Ahmad Mansour Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah represents the Al Sabah ruling family branch, having served as Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Defense from 2019-2020. The research shows no genealogical connections between these distinct regional family branches, each associated with their respective ruling dynasties and sovereign wealth management.
data.description: Multiple distinct Al Mansour family branches across Gulf states, most prominently the Al Nahyan branch in UAE where Mansour bin Zayed serves as vice president, with separate unrelated branches in Saudi Arabia's Al Saud family and Kuwait's Al Sabah family.
data.modernStatus: The UAE Al Nahyan branch controls major sovereign wealth funds including Mubadala Investment Company and Emirates Investment Authority, with Mansour bin Zayed owning Manchester City FC through City Football Group and holding stakes in various international investments including Virgin Galactic and Daimler.

data.history: The Al Ghannam family appears to be distributed across multiple regions of the Arabian Peninsula and Iraq, with documented presence in both the Najd region of Saudi Arabia and Iraq. One documented branch, represented by Iraqi Special Forces General Nasser al-Ghannam (born 1960), belongs to the Al-Ghannam branch of the Al-Shaya tribe, which is itself a branch of the larger Banu Uqayl tribe. Nasser bin Ahmed bin Ghannam was born in the city of Hit, Iraq, to Iraqi Arab parents, indicating an established Iraqi branch of the family. Historical records also show connections to notable Islamic scholarship through Husayn Ibn Abu Bakr Ibn Ghannam, a well-known Hanbali scholar and chronicler who studied under the sons of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab in Diriyah during the late 18th and early 19th centuries. This scholarly figure was from al-Ahsa and became an important chronicler of early Saudi and Wahhabi history, suggesting the family had educated members who played roles in documenting Arabian Peninsula history. The family name appears to have been present in the region during the formative period of the First Saudi State, with members involved in both military and scholarly pursuits across different parts of the Arabian Peninsula and Mesopotamia.
data.description: The Al Ghannam family has documented presence in Iraq and the Arabian Peninsula, with members serving in military leadership roles and Islamic scholarship, connected to the Al-Shaya tribe and Banu Uqayl confederation.
data.originStory: According to available records, at least one branch of Al Ghannam belongs to the Al-Shaya tribe, which is a branch of the larger Banu Uqayl tribe, though the specific geographical origins and migration patterns are not detailed in the research.
data.modernStatus: Family members have achieved prominent positions in Iraqi military leadership, with General Nasser al-Ghannam serving as dean of the Iraqi Military Academy from 2021 until 2025, holding various command positions including commander of multiple military divisions and operations.

data.history: Al Buraimi is not a traditional family name but rather refers to the inhabitants and historical community of the Buraimi Oasis, a strategically important region straddling the border between Oman and the UAE. The area, historically known as Tawam, consisted of nine villages and has been inhabited since ancient times, with evidence of settlement dating back to around 600 CE when it was part of the Sultanate of Oman. Around this time, Omani Azdi tribes took control of the region, though the city was abandoned around 700 CE and remained so for centuries. The original inhabitants were identified as the Al Nuaimi tribal group, particularly the Al-Naimi tribe, who rebuilt and ruled the settlement from the 1800s to the 1950s. The area was governed by various families in different periods: Al-Zar'a family controlled Al-Buraimi itself, Al-Surur family held authority over Hamasah, the Bani Jaber tribe controlled Sa'ara, and Al-Azaznah tribe governed Al Hailah village. The Al Hamoud family, part of the Al Nuaimi confederation, established significant rule under leaders such as Sultan bin Mohammed Al Hamoud Al Qurtasi Al Nuaimi, followed by Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan bin Mohammed Al Hamoud Al Qurtasi Al Nuaimi, with their rule persisting until 1950. The region gained international attention during the Buraimi Crisis (1949-1955), a territorial dispute between Saudi Arabia, Oman, and the UAE that was finally resolved through the Treaty of Jeddah in 1974, establishing the current borders and governance structure.
data.description: Al Buraimi refers to the historical community and inhabitants of the Buraimi Oasis, a border region between Oman and UAE. The area was traditionally governed by various tribal groups, most notably the Al Nuaimi confederation through the Al Hamoud family from the 1800s to 1950s.
data.originStory: The Buraimi Oasis has ancient roots dating to around 600 CE when it was part of the Sultanate of Oman. Omani Azdi tribes took control of the region during this period, though the settlement was abandoned around 700 CE. The area was later rebuilt and resettled by the Al Nuaimi tribal group, particularly the Al-Naimi tribe, who became the dominant inhabitants from the 1800s onward.
data.modernStatus: Al Buraimi is now the capital of Al Buraimi Governorate in Oman, established as a separate governorate in October 2006. It serves as a major commercial market and border crossing with the UAE, with Dr. Hamad bin Ahmed Al Busaidi serving as governor since 2020.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Buraimi is derived from the historical name of the oasis region, with the area also being known historically as 'Tawam' and 'Al Jaw'

data.history: The Al Awamiri family's documented history is primarily known through Ahmad al-'Awāmiri, who was selected as one of the founding members of the Academy of the Arabic Language in Cairo when it was established by King Fuad I of Egypt on December 31, 1932. King Fuad I personally played a major role in selecting the academy's members, choosing scholars who were modernists and politically moderate or neutral. Ahmad al-'Awāmiri was selected alongside other prominent academics, political figures, Azhari scholars, and orientalists to address urgent issues facing the Arabic language and adapt it to 20th century needs. The academy was inaugurated on January 30, 1934, with all members in attendance except one, indicating Ahmad al-'Awāmiri's participation in this significant cultural and linguistic institution in the Arab world.
data.description: The Al Awamiri family is documented through Ahmad al-'Awāmiri, who was a founding academic member of the Academy of the Arabic Language in Cairo, established by King Fuad I of Egypt in 1932. He was selected as part of a distinguished group of modernist scholars tasked with addressing linguistic challenges of the 20th century.

data.history: The Al Bu Hindi family is represented in the research by Ibrahim Bu Hindi (born 1948), a contemporary Bahraini journalist, poet, and playwright born in Muharraq, specifically in Farij al-Fadel district. Ibrahim Bu Hindi worked in the banking industry since 1968, serving as Assistant Director for Treasury and Investment at the National Bank of Bahrain and Kuwait. He became a prominent literary figure, serving as president of the Bahraini Family of Writers since April 30, 2018, and is a member of the Bahraini Awal Theater. His literary career began in the late 1960s during the expansion of the new Bahraini literary movement, writing poetry in both eloquent and colloquial forms that was published in Persian Gulf newspapers. He has five children: Rudayna, Juhayna, Muhannad, Munther, and Mohammad.
data.description: Al Bu Hindi is a Bahraini family known through Ibrahim Bu Hindi, a prominent journalist, poet, playwright, and banking professional from Muharraq who has led the Bahraini literary movement since the late 1960s.
data.modernStatus: The family is represented in modern Bahrain's cultural and literary circles through Ibrahim Bu Hindi, who serves as president of the Bahraini Family of Writers and has worked in senior banking positions.

data.history: The Al Menhali family appears to be a recognized Emirati family with members active in various fields including music and entertainment. Based on the research, Tariq Al Menhali is an Emirati singer who was born in Al Ain and later moved to Abu Dhabi at age six with his family. He became the first UAE academic artist to graduate from the Arab Oud House Academy. The family name Al Menhali appears in various contexts within UAE society, including among footballers like Mansoor Al-Menhali and Mohammed Al-Menhali who have played for UAE national teams. Additionally, Eida Al Menhali is mentioned as an Emirati Chaabi musician who collaborated with Diana Haddad and went on to become one of the most popular Emirati Chaabi musicians. The Al Menhali name also appears in the context of Bedouin Emirati tribes, being listed among prominent Bedouin tribes in the UAE alongside Al Shamsi, Al Ketbi, Al Ameri, and others.
data.description: The Al Menhali family is an Emirati family with notable presence in music, sports, and entertainment, including prominent musicians and footballers.
data.modernStatus: The family has members active in entertainment, music, and sports, with representatives in UAE national football teams and the music industry.

data.history: Al-Balushi (البلوشي) is a tribal surname common throughout the Arab Gulf states, representing an Arabized form of 'Balochi' or 'Baluchi' that denotes Baloch ancestry from Balochistan. Many families carrying this surname trace their ancestral origins to Balochistan, a region spanning southwestern Pakistan and southeastern Iran, with their ancestors primarily migrating from the Makran coast during the 18th century. The presence of Al-Balushis in Oman dates back centuries, rooted in historical connections between Makran (present-day Pakistan) and Oman through economic and political ties, particularly via Gwadar Port, which served as a crucial link between Arabia, South Asia, and colonial European powers. Gwadar remained under Omani rule for nearly two centuries until 1958, when Sultan Said bin Taimur sold the territory to Pakistan for $3 million. Today, Oman hosts one of the largest Al-Balushi populations outside Pakistan and Iran, with an estimated 35% of Oman's population being of Al-Balushi origin. The majority of Al-Balushis speak Arabic while some retain their native Balochi language, and they are predominantly Sunni Muslims.
data.description: Al-Balushi is a widespread Gulf tribal surname of Baloch origin, with families tracing ancestry to 18th-century migrations from the Makran coast of Balochistan. They form significant populations across Oman, UAE, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, and Eastern Saudi Arabia.
data.originStory: Al-Balushi families trace their origins to Balochistan, a region in southwestern Pakistan and southeastern Iran, with ancestors primarily migrating from the Makran coast during the 18th century due to historical economic and political connections between Makran and the Arabian Peninsula.
data.modernStatus: Al-Balushis are well-integrated across Gulf societies, with notable representation in sports, politics, entertainment, and professional fields. They maintain cultural connections while being predominantly Arabic-speaking and form substantial populations, particularly in Oman where they comprise an estimated 35% of the population.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Balushi is an Arabized form of the term Balochi or Baluchi, denoting Baloch ancestry from Balochistan

migrationPath.description: Primary migration from Makran coast to Arabian Peninsula during 18th century

data.history: The Al Gichki family are descendants of Rajput horsemen who migrated to the Makran region of present-day Pakistan and Iran between the 15th-17th centuries. According to research, their ancestor Jagat Singh migrated from Rajputana in the 17th century, with suggested origins including Jaipur, Jodhpur, Marwar in Rajasthan, Jamnagar in Gujarat, Lahore in Punjab, and Sindh. They converted to Islam three generations after their arrival and adopted the Zikri faith. The Gichki established themselves as rulers through diplomacy and alliances rather than through pastoral nomadism, likely arriving as a group of mercenaries numbering forty Rajput horsemen according to local tradition. They initially allied with the Buledi tribe to defeat the ruler of Kech, Malik Mirza, but later ousted the Buledi to gain complete control over Makran after 1740 under their chief Mulla Murad. The family ruled the Makran region as Nawabs from 1740 until 1955, with two major branches - Isazai ruling Panjgur and Dinarzai ruling Kech. During the rule of Malik Dinar Gichki, Makran was invaded nine times by Nasir Khan Brahui of Kalat, forcing the Gichki to pay half their revenue as tribute. The advent of British rule weakened Kalat's influence, allowing the Gichki Nawabs to reassert their independence. The last Nawab, Mir Baian Gichki, acceded the state to Pakistan in 1947, and the princely state was dissolved in 1955.
data.description: Al Gichki is a Muslim Rajput family that ruled the princely state of Makran from 1740-1955, originally migrating from Rajputana in the 17th century and establishing control over the Makran coastal region of present-day Pakistan through diplomatic alliances.
data.originStory: According to locally well-known tradition from Makran, the ancestors of Gichki were forty Rajput horsemen from the north who migrated to Makran between the 15th-17th centuries. Their ancestor Jagat Singh had migrated from Rajputana in the 17th century and converted to Islam. They are stated to be Rajputs with probable places of origin including Jaipur, Jodhpur, Marwar in Rajasthan, Jamnagar in Gujarat, Lahore in Punjab, and Sindh.
data.modernStatus: The Al Gichki family's political rule ended in 1955 when the princely state of Makran was dissolved and incorporated into Pakistan. They now identify as Baloch despite their Rajput origins and speak Balochi. Their former castles in Kech display architecture blending their Rajput heritage with Persian cultural influences.

Soon conflict broke out between the two tribes and Gichki ousted Buledi as well, hence gaining complete control over Makran.

data.history: The Chandio is a Baloch tribe present in the Sindh and Balochistan provinces of Pakistan. The tribe follows a traditional tribal system with their tribal leader, Nawab Ghaibi Sardar Khan Chandio. The research does not provide specific information about their historical origins, migration patterns, or founding stories. Notable members of the tribe have been involved in Pakistani politics and civil society, with several individuals achieving prominence in various fields including law, entertainment, and politics. The family has produced activists like Umme Rubab Chandio, who gained national attention for her fight against feudalism after her father, grandfather, and uncle were assassinated in 2018.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Hooth (Hooth) family appears to be of Baluchi origin with historical connections to Oman. The most significant historical reference places members of the Hooth tribe in positions of military command during the Portuguese colonial period in Oman in the late 16th and early 17th centuries. According to legend preserved in Omani sources, two Baluchi commanders from the Hooth tribe - Mir Jalal Khan and his brother Mir Miran - served as military leaders, with Al Jalali Fort and Fort Al-Mirani in Muscat's harbor reportedly named after them respectively. This suggests the Hooth tribe had established themselves as a prominent military family in the region during this period. In modern times, the family maintains political prominence, with Abdul Malik Baloch, a member of the Hooth tribe, serving as the 21st Chief Minister of Balochistan, Pakistan from June 2013 to December 2015. He was notably the first non-tribal leader to hold this position and led efforts to combat corruption while improving infrastructure in the province.
data.description: The Al Hooth is a Baluchi tribe with historical military prominence in Oman and modern political influence in Pakistan, particularly in Balochistan province.
data.originStory: The family traces its origins to the Baluchi people, with historical presence documented in both Oman and the broader Balochistan region spanning modern-day Pakistan and Iran.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains political prominence in Pakistan, with Abdul Malik Baloch serving as Chief Minister of Balochistan (2013-2015) and as president of the National Party.

folkLegends.story: According to Omani legend, Al Jalali Fort was named after Mir Jalal Khan, a Baluchi commander from the Hooth tribe, while the twin Fort Al-Mirani was named after his brother Mir Miran, who was also a commander. Both served during the Portuguese colonial period.

data.nameEtymology: The name 'Hooth' appears to be the tribal designation, with 'Al' being the Arabic definite article meaning 'the' or indicating 'family of'.

data.history:
The Al Azmi family name appears to be primarily associated with the Azamgarh district of Uttar Pradesh, India, where it developed as a toponymic surname among Indian Muslims who originated from or had connections to the city of Azamgarh. Azamgarh was founded in 1665 by Azam, son of Vikramajit, a descendant of Gautam Rajputs who had embraced Islam. The city became a significant center of Islamic scholarship and Urdu literature in northern India.

The Al Azmi surname spread beyond India through various migration patterns. The research shows individuals with this surname in Kuwait, where they have held political positions, and in other regions. Some members of the Al Azmi family became prominent in various fields including literature, politics, and Islamic scholarship. The Kuwaiti branch includes political figures like Lieutenant Colonel Khaled Niga al-Azmi and politicians such as Marzouq Al-Hubaini Al-Azmi.

The family's modern presence spans multiple countries and professions. In India, the Azmi name is associated with prominent figures in Urdu poetry and cinema, including Kaifi Azmi (1919-2002), the renowned Urdu poet who was part of the Progressive Writers' Movement, and his daughter Shabana Azmi, the acclaimed actress. The family has maintained connections to Islamic scholarship, with figures like Qamaruzzaman Azmi serving as prominent Islamic scholars and educators.
data.description: A family name primarily originating from Azamgarh, India, that has spread across various regions including Kuwait, associated with literature, politics, and Islamic scholarship.
data.originStory: The Al Azmi surname derives from the city of Azamgarh in Uttar Pradesh, India, which was founded in 1665 by Azam, son of Vikramajit. The name became a toponymic surname adopted by people with connections to this city.
data.modernStatus: The Al Azmi family members are found in various professions across multiple countries, including politics in Kuwait, Islamic scholarship, and arts in India. They maintain presence in academic, political, and cultural spheres.
data.nameEtymology: Al Azmi is a toponymic surname derived from Azamgarh city, which was named after its founder Azam in 1665

migrationPath.description: Settlement and adoption of Azamgarh-based surname

data.history: The Al Suwailem family is documented through historical records related to the town of Al-Bukayriyah in Al-Qassim Province, Saudi Arabia. According to Wikipedia's entry on Al-Bukayriyah, when the waters of al-Dhalafa northwest of the Mulaida region came after the middle of the twelfth century AH (approximately mid-18th century CE), the majority of its people from the Areenat branch of the Subay tribe were dispersed, leaving their properties and homes. Among those who remained was Othman bin Imran Al-Arini, who is documented as the grandfather of several families including Al Suwailem, along with the families of Dakhil Allah, Al Khudair, and Al Omair. Othman bin Imran Al-Arini purchased Al-Bukayriyah from its original owner Al-Bukairi, and they built the settlement there. The family name also appears in modern times through Samir Salih Abdullah al-Suwaylim (1969-2002), commonly known as Ibn al-Khattab, a Saudi-born militant who was involved in various conflicts including the Chechen Wars. Born in Arar, Saudi Arabia, he was described as coming from a Bedouin father of the Arab Suwaylim tribe and a mother of Syrian Turkmen descent. Additionally, historical records from the walled town of Riyadh mention Al-Suwailem Gate as one of the gates in the city fortifications, and Al-Suwailem Street in the Duhairah quarter, indicating the family's presence in the historic capital.
data.description: Al Suwailem is a documented Arab family with historical ties to Al-Qassim Province, particularly Al-Bukayriyah, descended from the Subay tribe through the Areenat branch. The family has maintained presence in both Al-Qassim and Riyadh regions of Saudi Arabia.
data.originStory: The family traces its documented lineage to Othman bin Imran Al-Arini, who lived after the middle of the twelfth century AH (mid-18th century CE). When the original settlement of al-Dhalafa was abandoned due to water issues, Othman remained and purchased the area that became Al-Bukayriyah from its owner Al-Bukairi, establishing the family's presence there.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains historical significance in Saudi Arabia, with documented presence in both Al-Qassim Province and Riyadh. Notable modern figure includes Samir Salih Abdullah al-Suwaylim (Ibn al-Khattab), though his activities were militant in nature.

data.history: The Al Mojel family's documented modern presence in Saudi Arabia's industrial and financial sectors is evidenced through their leadership role in government institutions. Dr. Ibrahim Almojel currently serves as Chief Executive Officer of the Saudi Industrial Development Fund (SIDF), a significant government financial institution established in 1974. Under his leadership, the fund has expanded its mandate from solely financing local manufacturing businesses to include energy, logistics, and mining projects starting in 2019. The family's involvement in this key institution demonstrates their integration into Saudi Arabia's economic development strategy, particularly as it relates to Vision 2030 initiatives.
data.description: The Al Mojel family is represented in Saudi Arabia's industrial development sector through Dr. Ibrahim Almojel's leadership of the Saudi Industrial Development Fund. Their role reflects involvement in the kingdom's economic diversification efforts.
data.modernStatus: The family holds a senior executive position in Saudi Arabia's government financial sector, with Dr. Ibrahim Almojel serving as CEO of the Saudi Industrial Development Fund, which manages over US$2.4 billion in appropriations and oversees major industrial development projects.

data.history: Al Jasser (Arabic: الجاسر) is an Arabic surname commonly found in Saudi Arabia, with the family name appearing across various fields including politics, economics, journalism, and entertainment. The research reveals several prominent members but does not establish a single unified family lineage or common origin story. Notable figures include Dr. Muhammad bin Sulaiman Al-Jasser (born 1955), a prominent Saudi economist who served as governor of the Saudi Arabian Central Bank (SAMA) and minister of economy and planning, and Turki bin Abdulaziz al-Jasser (died 2025), a Saudi journalist and blogger who was executed for his online activities. The family name also appears among Syrian-origin individuals, such as Mohamed Zuhdi Jasser (born 1967), an American physician of Syrian descent whose grandfather owned a vegetable oil company in Syria before the family migrated to the United States in the 1960s due to political repression.
data.description: Al Jasser is an Arabic surname found across the Arab world, particularly in Saudi Arabia and among Syrian diaspora communities, with notable members in economics, politics, journalism, and medicine.
data.modernStatus: The Al-Jasser family includes prominent figures in Saudi government and business, including former central bank governors and cabinet ministers, as well as diaspora communities in the United States engaged in medical practice and political commentary.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Mashari family appears to be one of the cadet branches of the House of Saud, the ruling royal family of Saudi Arabia. According to Wikipedia sources on the House of Saud, the surname 'Al Saud' is carried by any descendant of Muhammad bin Saud or his three brothers Farhan, Thunayyan, and Mishari. The Al Mashari branch specifically derives from Mishari, one of these founding brothers of the Saudi dynasty. The House of Saud itself originates from the Mrudah clan, which settled in Diriyah in 1446-1447 under their leader Mani' ibn Rabiah Al-Muraydi. The Mrudah are believed to be descended from the Banu Hanifa branch of the larger Rabi'ah tribal confederation. The Al Mashari, as a cadet branch, would traditionally hold high and influential positions in government though they are not in the direct line of succession to the Saudi throne. The research indicates that many cadet branch members intermarry within the Al Saud family to re-establish their lineage and continue to wield influence in government.
data.description: The Al Mashari are a cadet branch of the House of Saud, descended from Mishari, one of the three brothers of the dynasty's founder Muhammad bin Saud. As a cadet branch, they traditionally hold influential government positions while remaining outside the direct line of royal succession.
data.originStory: The Al Mashari trace their origins to Mishari, one of the three brothers of Muhammad bin Saud, the 18th century founder of the Saudi dynasty. The broader family originates from the Mrudah clan, which settled in Diriyah in 1446-1447 under Mani' ibn Rabiah Al-Muraydi.

data.modernStatus: Cadet branch of the House of Saud holding high and influential positions in Saudi government, though not in direct line of succession to the throne

data.history: The Al Turki Al Sheikh represents two distinct but notable families in the Gulf region. The Al ash-Sheikh family, from which several prominent figures derive their lineage, has deep roots in Saudi Arabia's religious establishment as descendants of Muhammad bin Abdul Wahhab, the founder of the Wahhabi movement. This family has produced many prominent Saudi religious leaders and has intermarried with the ruling Al Saud family, creating powerful political alliances. Prince Turki bin Faisal Al Saud, born in 1945, represents the intersection of royal and religious lineages through his mother Iffat bint Mohammad Al Thunayan and his position within the Al Saud hierarchy. Separately, Turki bin Abdul Mohsen Al-Sheikh (Turki Alalshikh), born in 1981, belongs to the Al ash-Sheikh family which holds second place in prestige after the ruling dynasty in Saudi Arabia. His rise to power came through his association with Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, starting from their shared interests in video games and evolving into significant government positions including chairman of the General Entertainment Authority. Both figures demonstrate the continued influence of established families in modern Gulf politics and society, though through different pathways - one through traditional royal succession and diplomatic service, the other through modern political alliance and entertainment industry leadership.
data.description: The Al Turki Al Sheikh encompasses notable Saudi families with religious and political prominence, including descendants of Wahhabi founder Muhammad bin Abdul Wahhab and modern government officials who have shaped Saudi Arabia's diplomatic, intelligence, and entertainment sectors.
data.originStory: The Al ash-Sheikh family traces its origins to Muhammad bin Abdul Wahhab, the 18th-century religious leader who founded the Wahhabi movement. This religious lineage became intertwined with the Al Saud ruling family through strategic marriages and political alliances, establishing their position as the second most prestigious family in Saudi Arabia after the royal dynasty.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant influence in modern Saudi Arabia through high-level government positions. Prince Turki bin Faisal served as director of Saudi intelligence (1979-2001) and ambassador to the UK and US, while currently chairing the King Faisal Foundation's Center for Research and Islamic Studies. Turki Alalshikh holds the position of chairman of the General Entertainment Authority and serves as a royal advisor with ministerial rank, playing a key role in Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 entertainment sector transformation.

data.history: The Al Bunyan family's documented history centers on Ayad Bunyan, who served as president of Al-Shorta Sports Club in Iraq. His daughter Hawra Ayad Bunyan al-Nadawi was born in Baghdad in August 1984 to Arab and Kurdish parents. Both of Hawra's parents were political prisoners under Saddam Hussein's regime, and as an infant, she stayed in Iraqi jails with her mother. The family fled Iraq for Denmark after the 1991 uprising when Hawra was six years old, settling in Copenhagen where she grew up speaking Arabic, Kurdish, and Danish. Hawra became a prominent writer, with her debut novel 'Under the Copenhagen Sky' being longlisted for the 2012 Arabic Booker Prize, making her the youngest and only female nominee that year. She later studied linguistics and moved to London, marrying footballer Nashat Akram who had played for Al-Shorta Sports Club during her father's presidency.
data.description: An Iraqi family with Arab and Kurdish heritage, known for Ayad Bunyan's role as Al-Shorta Sports Club president and his daughter Hawra's literary achievements as an Arabic Booker Prize nominee.
data.originStory: The family originates from Iraq, with documented Arab and Kurdish ethnic heritage. They were based in Baghdad before being forced into exile due to political persecution under Saddam Hussein's regime.
data.modernStatus: The family is now dispersed internationally, with members in Denmark and London. Hawra al-Nadawi has established herself as a prominent Arabic writer and linguist, while maintaining connections to Iraqi sports through family ties to Al-Shorta Sports Club.

data.history: The Saigh family appears in the historical record through Frederick Michael Saigh Jr. (1905-1999), a prominent American businessman of Lebanese descent. Born in Springfield, Illinois, Saigh was the son of Lebanese immigrants who owned a chain of grocery stores in the Midwest. He became a highly successful tax and corporate lawyer in St. Louis, eventually accumulating substantial real estate holdings including landmark buildings like the Railway Exchange Building and Scruggs-Vandervoort-Barney building. Saigh's most notable achievement was his ownership of the St. Louis Cardinals baseball team from 1948 to 1953, purchasing the franchise for $4 million from longtime owner Sam Breadon. However, his ownership ended abruptly when he was convicted of federal tax evasion in 1953, serving six months in federal prison. Under pressure from Major League Baseball Commissioner Ford Frick, Saigh sold the Cardinals to Anheuser-Busch for $3.75 million, reportedly choosing civic pride over higher offers from out-of-town buyers who would have moved the team. After his release, Saigh resumed his business career and became the largest Anheuser-Busch shareholder outside the Busch family, amassing approximately $500 million by his death in 1999. He left $70 million to charity through the Fred Saigh Foundation, having had no children with his wife Elizabeth.
data.description: Lebanese-American family known primarily through Frederick Saigh Jr., a successful St. Louis lawyer and businessman who owned the Cardinals baseball team from 1948-1953 before selling due to tax evasion conviction.
data.originStory: The Saigh family originated from Lebanon, with Frederick Saigh Jr.'s parents immigrating to the United States where they established a chain of grocery stores in the Midwest.
data.modernStatus: The family's prominence was centered on Frederick Saigh Jr.'s business empire in St. Louis real estate and brief ownership of the Cardinals. After his death in 1999, the family legacy continues through the Fred Saigh Foundation established with his $70 million bequest.
data.nameEtymology: The surname is pronounced 'sigh' according to historical records, though specific etymological origins are not detailed in available sources.

data.history: The Pahlavi dynasty, founded by Reza Khan (later Reza Shah Pahlavi) in 1925, was the last ruling monarchy of Iran. Reza Khan was of Mazandarani origin, born in Alasht, Savadkuh County, Mazandaran Province, and served as a Brigadier-General in the Persian Cossack Brigade. After a military coup in 1921, he deposed the last Qajar shah in 1925 and established the Pahlavi dynasty, choosing the surname 'Pahlavi' (the name for Middle Persian language) when Iran required all citizens to adopt surnames. His eldest son Mohammad Reza Pahlavi ascended to the throne in 1941 after his father was forced to abdicate during the Anglo-Soviet invasion of Iran. Mohammad Reza ruled for 37 years until he was overthrown in the 1979 Islamic Revolution, spending his final years in exile before dying in Egypt in 1980. The dynasty ended with the establishment of the Islamic Republic of Iran under Ayatollah Khomeini.
data.description: The Pahlavi dynasty was Iran's last royal house (1925-1979), founded by Reza Khan of Mazandarani origin and ending with his son Mohammad Reza Pahlavi's overthrow in the Islamic Revolution.
data.originStory: Reza Khan, a military officer from Mazandaran Province who rose from private to Brigadier-General in the Persian Cossack Brigade, seized power in a 1921 coup and established himself as Shah in 1925, founding the Pahlavi dynasty.
data.modernStatus: The Pahlavi dynasty ended in 1979 with the Islamic Revolution. Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi, son of the last Shah, founded and leads the National Council of Iran, a government in exile.
data.nameEtymology: Pahlavi is the name for the Middle Persian language, derived from Old Persian. Reza Khan chose this surname when Iran required all citizens to adopt family names.

data.history: The research provided contains no information about a GCC family named 'Shaker'. The search results discuss three unrelated topics: Shaker furniture made by the United Society of Believers in Christ's Second Appearing (a religious sect), Shaker Aamer (a Saudi citizen detained at Guantanamo Bay), and various other entities with 'Shaker' in their names, but none relate to a Gulf Cooperation Council family lineage. The Shakers mentioned were an American religious community founded in England around 1747, who developed a distinctive furniture style and practiced celibacy, communal living, and pacifism. Shaker Aamer was born in Saudi Arabia in 1966 but lived in Britain and was detained by US forces in Afghanistan in 2001. data.history: The Al Hashim family refers to the Hashemite royal family of Jordan, officially known as the House of Hashim, which has ruled Jordan since 1921. The family belongs to the Dhawu Awn, one of the branches of the Ḥasanid Sharifs of Mecca, also referred to as Hashemites. Their eponymous ancestor is traditionally considered to be Hashim ibn Abd Manaf, great-grandfather of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. The current dynasty was founded by Sharif Hussein ibn Ali, who was appointed as Sharif and Emir of Mecca by the Ottoman sultan Abdul Hamid II in 1908, then in 1916 was proclaimed King of Arab countries after initiating the Arab Revolt against the Ottoman Empire. His sons Abdullah and Faisal assumed the thrones of Jordan and Iraq in 1921, in what became known as the 'Sharifian solution'. Abdullah was assassinated in 1951, but his descendants continue to rule Jordan today as the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan.
data.description: The Hashemite royal family of Jordan, claiming descent from the Islamic prophet Muhammad through Hashim ibn Abd Manaf, who have ruled Jordan since 1921 and previously ruled Mecca for centuries.
data.originStory: According to Islamic tradition, the Hashemites trace their lineage to Hashim ibn Abd Manaf (died c. 497 AD), the great-grandfather of Muhammad. The family had ruled the city of Mecca continuously from the 10th century, frequently as vassals of outside powers. In c. 968, Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Hasani came from Medina and conquered Mecca in the name of the Fatimid caliph, establishing Hashemite rule over the holy city.
data.tribalOrigin: Banu Hashim (clan within the Quraysh tribe)
data.modernStatus: The Hashemite family currently rules the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan, with King Abdullah II on the throne since 1999. The family maintains custodianship over Muslim and Christian holy sites in Jerusalem and continues to play a significant role in Middle Eastern politics.
data.nameEtymology: Named after Hashim ibn Abd Manaf, great-grandfather of the Islamic prophet Muhammad

data.history: The research provides information about three distinct individuals with the surname Al Yamani, but does not establish them as members of a single family lineage. Ahmed al-Hasan al-Yamani is an Iraqi Shia religious leader born in Basra who claims descent from Muhammad al-Mahdi through his paternal line: son of Ismael, son of Saleh, son of Hussain, son of Salman. His uncle Muhsin ibn Saleh and tribal sheikhs testify to this genealogical claim tracing back to the twelfth Imam. Abd al-Rahman al-Mu'allimi al-Yamani was a Yemeni Islamic scholar born in 1894 in Al-Mahaqirah village near Sanaa, who became a prominent Salafi scholar and moved between Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Indonesia, and India throughout his career. The surname 'al-Yamani' in Ahmed al-Hasan's case refers to his interpretation that all members of the Prophet's family are 'Yamanis' because Mecca is from Touhama, which is from Yemen, rather than indicating Yemeni geographic origin. No family connections are established between these individuals in the research.
data.description: Al Yamani appears in the research as a surname used by distinct individuals from Iraq and Yemen, with no established family connections between them.
data.nameEtymology: According to Ahmed al-Hasan's explanation, 'Yamani' refers to the fact that Mecca is from Touhama which is from Yemen, making all members of the Prophet's family 'Yamanis' rather than indicating geographic Yemeni origin

data.history: The al-Husseini family is a prominent Palestinian Arab noble family from Jerusalem with origins tracing back to the Islamic prophet Muhammad. The family consisted of wealthy landowners in southern Palestine, centered around the district of Jerusalem, with thirteen members serving as Mayors of Jerusalem between 1864 and 1920. The family produced several influential religious and political leaders, including multiple Muftis of Jerusalem. During the British Mandate period, the al-Husseinis became deeply involved in Palestinian nationalism and resistance to Zionism, with Amin al-Husseini serving as Grand Mufti of Jerusalem from 1921 and becoming a central figure in Palestinian politics. The family's influence extended through various religious and political institutions, including the Supreme Muslim Council, which controlled significant waqf funds and Islamic courts in Palestine. Following the 1948 Arab-Israeli War and subsequent conflicts, family members were dispersed, with some continuing political activism while others focused on educational and cultural institutions like Dar al-Tifl al-Arabi founded by Hind al-Husseini.
data.description: A prominent Palestinian Arab noble family from Jerusalem with historical ties to the Islamic prophet Muhammad, known for producing mayors, muftis, and political leaders who played central roles in Palestinian nationalism and resistance movements.
data.originStory: The al-Husayni family of Jerusalemite Arab nobles trace their origins to the Islamic prophet Muhammad, according to the research.
data.modernStatus: The family remains influential in Palestinian society through educational institutions like Dar al-Tifl al-Arabi and various cultural organizations, though their direct political influence has diminished since the establishment of the PLO in 1964.

The research focuses entirely on medieval and modern Islamic scholars and their biographical details, theological contributions, and historical contexts.
