data.history:
The Al Bosnawi family traces its documented origins to 16th-century Bosnia under the Ottoman Empire, specifically to the area of Sudići near Foča in eastern Bosnia. The family's most prominent historical figure was Ahmed Sudi, also known as Sudi-yi Bosnawi or Ahmed-i Bosnawi, a renowned Ottoman Turkish scholar and commentator on Persian classics. Born in Sudići near Foča, Ahmed Sudi received his early education locally before moving to Sarajevo and eventually to Constantinople (present-day Istanbul) to pursue advanced studies, following the typical educational path of Bosnian scholars of his era.

Ahmed Sudi's scholarly journey took him throughout the Ottoman Empire, where he studied under notable teachers including the Sunni Iranian emigré Mosleh al-Din Lari in Amed (present-day Diyarbakir). He traveled extensively through Damascus, Baghdad, Najaf, and Kufa, and undertook the Hajj pilgrimage. Throughout his travels, he dedicated himself to mastering Persian literature and language, engaging with both scholars and Iranian merchants to deepen his understanding of Persian poetry and literature.

Returning to Constantinople, Ahmed Sudi became a teacher at the Ibrahim Pasha madrasa and authored several influential Ottoman Turkish commentaries on Persian classics, including works on Rumi's Masnavi, Saadi Shirazi's Gulistan and Bostan, and Hafez's Divān. His commentary on Hafez's divan, completed in 1594, was considered of such exceptional quality that it remained the standard reference work for centuries. According to Professor Hamid Algar, Sudi became 'perhaps the most prominent of all Ottoman Persianists.' He died between 1591-1598 and was buried in the Yusuf Pasha mosque in Aksaray, Constantinople.
data.description: The Al Bosnawi family originated in 16th-century Bosnia under the Ottoman Empire, producing Ahmed Sudi, one of the most prominent Ottoman Persian scholars and commentators. The family name derives from their Bosnian origins.
data.originStory: The Al Bosnawi family originated in Sudići near Foča in eastern Bosnia during the Ottoman period. Ahmed Sudi, born in this region, followed the established path of Bosnian scholars by moving first to Sarajevo, then to Constantinople for advanced education, taking advantage of the presence of fellow Bosnian Sokollu Mehmed Pasha as Ottoman Grand Vizier.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Al Bosnawi' derives from Bosnia, with Ahmed Sudi's nisba 'Sudi' coming from his birthplace Sudići near Foča in eastern Bosnia.

data.history: The Al Sudani family traces its origins to the province of Maysan in southern Iraq, with the name 'al-Sudani' indicating their connection to Sudan or possibly meaning 'the black one' in Arabic. The family's documented history centers around Mohammed Shia' al-Sudani, born in Baghdad on March 4, 1970, to a middle-class Shia Arab family originally from Maysan province. His father worked as an employee at the Agricultural Cooperative Bank of Iraq but was executed along with five other family members when Mohammed was around 10 years old for being members of the Islamic Dawa Party, which opposed Saddam Hussein's Ba'athist rule. This tragedy profoundly shaped the family's political trajectory and opposition to the Ba'athist regime. Following the 2003 invasion of Iraq, the family emerged as significant political actors, with Mohammed Shia' al-Sudani rising through various governmental positions to become Prime Minister of Iraq in 2022. The family also produced Harith al-Sudani (1981-2017), an intelligence officer who became Iraq's most successful spy against ISIS, infiltrating the terrorist organization and preventing approximately 30 car-bomb attacks before being captured and executed in 2017.
data.description: An Iraqi Shia Arab family from Maysan province that rose to political prominence after 2003, producing Iraq's current Prime Minister Mohammed Shia' al-Sudani and the celebrated intelligence officer Harith al-Sudani who died fighting ISIS.
data.originStory: The Al Sudani family originates from the province of Maysan in southern Iraq, with Mohammed Shia' al-Sudani's family moving from Maysan to Baghdad where he was born in 1970. The family's political awakening came through tragedy when Mohammed's father and five other family members were executed by Saddam Hussein's regime for membership in the banned Islamic Dawa Party.
data.modernStatus: The Al Sudani family currently holds the highest political office in Iraq, with Mohammed Shia' al-Sudani serving as Prime Minister since October 2022 and acting Minister of Defense since 2026. He leads the Furatayn Movement political party and the Reconstruction and Development Coalition electoral alliance.
data.nameEtymology: The surname 'al-Sudani' likely indicates a connection to Sudan or could mean 'the black one' in Arabic, though the specific etymological origin is not definitively established in the available sources.

data.history:
The Al Attar family name derives from the Arabic word 'Attar' (عطار), meaning herbalist, druggist, perfumist or alchemist. This occupational designation was historically used by those involved in medicine and drugs based on herbs, similar to modern-day town doctors and pharmacists. The term also refers to rose oil production. The most famous historical bearer of this name was Faridoddin Attar Nishapuri (c. 1145 – c. 1221), a Persian poet and Sufi mystic from Nishapur who practiced as a pharmacist and attended to numerous customers. His profession as an apothecary gave him the pen-name 'Attar,' which he used throughout his literary career.

In the modern Arab world, the Al Attar name appears across different regions and contexts. In Syria, the family has produced notable political figures, including Najah al-Attar, who served as Vice President from 2006 to 2024 and was the first Arab woman to hold such a position. Born in Damascus in 1933 to a Sunni Muslim family, her father was among the first Arab nationalist leaders who participated in the 1925-1927 Syrian revolt against the French Mandate. Interestingly, her brother Issam al-Attar was the leader of the Damascus faction of the Syrian Muslim Brotherhood and lived in exile, demonstrating the political complexity within the family.

The name also extends to other regions, with Princess Rajwa Al Hussein (born Rajwa Al Saif) having family connections to the Al-Attar region in Saudi Arabia's Sudair area. The Al Saif family, into which she was born, descends from the sheikhs of the town of Al-Attar in the Sudair region, which historically belonged to the Subai tribe. This indicates that the Al Attar designation has both occupational and geographical significance, representing both those who practiced the trade of herbalism and those who originated from specific locations bearing this name.
data.description: Al Attar is an occupational surname meaning herbalist or pharmacist, with notable families across the Arab world including Syrian political figures and connections to Saudi tribal regions.
data.originStory: The name Al Attar originates from the Arabic occupational term meaning herbalist, druggist, perfumist or alchemist, historically used by those who practiced medicine using herbs, similar to modern pharmacists and town doctors.
data.tribalOrigin: Subai tribe (for the Al-Attar region in Saudi Arabia's Sudair)
data.modernStatus: The Al Attar name is associated with prominent political figures in Syria, including former Vice President Najah al-Attar, and has geographical connections to Saudi Arabia through the Al-Attar region in Sudair.
data.nameEtymology: From Arabic 'Attar' (عطار) meaning herbalist, druggist, perfumist or alchemist; also refers to rose oil

data.history: The Al Awsi family name derives from the ancient Banu Aws tribe, one of the two main Arab tribes of Medina during the early Islamic period. The Banu Aws were prominently involved in the foundational events of Islam, including the Battle of Badr in 624 CE where 61 men from the Aws participated in Muhammad's army of 313 fighters. The tribe was one of the Ansar (helpers) who supported Muhammad after his migration to Medina and played crucial roles in early Islamic military campaigns. Historical records show the Al-Awsi nisba (family designation) appearing in medieval Islamic scholarship, notably with Ibn Al-Raqqam Muhammad Ibn Ibrahim Al-Mursi Al-Andalusi Al-Tunisi Al-Awsi, a 13th-century Andalusian-Arab astronomer and mathematician born in Murcia in 1250. This suggests the Al Awsi lineage spread across the Islamic world during the medieval period, from the Arabian Peninsula to Al-Andalus and North Africa, following patterns of Islamic expansion and scholarly migration.
data.description: Al Awsi is a family lineage tracing back to the ancient Banu Aws tribe of Medina, one of the key Arab tribes that supported the Prophet Muhammad. The name appears in medieval Islamic scholarship and maintains connections to early Islamic history.
data.originStory: The Al Awsi family traces its origins to the Banu Aws tribe, one of the two main Arab tribes of pre-Islamic Medina (alongside the Banu Khazraj) who became the Ansar (helpers) supporting the Prophet Muhammad after his migration from Mecca.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Awsi derives from 'Aws', the name of the ancient Arabian tribe, with the nisba (attributive) suffix indicating descent or affiliation with the Banu Aws tribe of Medina.

data.history: The Al Jaziri family has deep historical roots in Tunisia, with documented presence dating back to the 12th-18th centuries. The family owned Dar Al Jaziri, a palace in the medina of Tunis located on Tribunal Street, which served as one of their residences during this extended period. The palace, along with the family mausoleum known as Tourbet Al Jaziri, demonstrates the family's established position in Tunisian society over several centuries. In 1937, the palace housed the second Neo Destour congress, indicating the family's continued significance in Tunisian political and social circles. The family's influence extended into the modern era, with members like Fadhel Jaziri (1948-2025) achieving prominence in the arts. Born in Tunis to an upper-middle-class family that operated a fez-making business and other hospitality-related enterprises, Fadhel became a renowned actor and film director. His father owned a bookshop in the Bab Souika neighborhood, which became a meeting place for artists, theater personalities, and political figures, positioning the family at the center of Tunis's cultural and intellectual life.
data.description: A historic Tunisian family with documented presence in Tunis from the 12th-18th centuries, known for their palace Dar Al Jaziri and their involvement in business, culture, and the arts, including the renowned actor-director Fadhel Jaziri.
data.originStory: The Al Jaziri family has been established in Tunisia with their primary residence being the historic Dar Al Jaziri palace in the medina of Tunis, though the research does not specify their ultimate origins or migration story to Tunisia.
data.modernStatus: The family maintained prominence into the 21st century through cultural contributions, particularly in theater and film. Fadhel Jaziri was politically active as a member of the Economic and Social Commission of Nidaa Tounes party until his resignation in 2016.

data.history: The Al Farooq (Arabic: فاروق) family are a Sunni Muslim business family originally from Saudi Arabia, belonging to the Al Ansar tribe, whose ancestral home later became Bastak in the Hormozgan province of southern Iran around 1200 Hijri. The family was part of the first wave of migration from Bastak starting in 1899, led by Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil, due to the introduction of customs tax by the Persian government under the Qajar dynasty which reduced merchant profits. Political instability in 1909 and a flu epidemic in 1918 saw most merchant families from Bastak relocate to the Persian Gulf region, including Dubai. After Mohammed Aqil's death, his sons Mohammed Farooq (known as 'The Great Farooq') and Ahmed Al Arshi expanded the business internationally, trading primarily in diamonds, pearls and precious stones. The Great Farooq's generosity and hospitality earned him respect among Gulf royalty, including close ties with Sheikh Mohammed Bin Saqer Al Qasimi of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, and Sheikh Saeed Bin Maktoum Al Maktoum of Dubai. Following Mohammed Farooq's death, his son Abdulkarim Farooq became heir to the family legacy, serving as advisor to Gulf rulers including Sheikh Rashid Bin Saeed Al Maktoum. Abdulkarim owned the largest general trading establishment in Al Bastakiya called London Store, specializing in importing quality brands from London, and was regarded as the wealthiest man in Dubai. The family established landmarks including the Farooq Mosque in Al Bastakiya and Bayt Faruq in Bahrain, and are recognized as one of the wealthiest families in the Persian Gulf region.
data.description: A Sunni Muslim merchant family originally from Saudi Arabia's Al Ansar tribe, who settled in Bastak, Iran before migrating to Dubai in 1899. They became one of the UAE's first business and aristocratic families, specializing in pearls, diamonds and precious stones trading.
data.originStory: The Al Farooq family originally belonged to the Al Ansar tribe in Saudi Arabia before settling in Bastak in the Hormozgan province of southern Iran around 1200 Hijri. They migrated from Bastak to Dubai starting in 1899 due to Persian government customs taxes that reduced merchant profits.
data.modernStatus: As of 2014, the majority of family members reside in the UAE, Bahrain, Kuwait, United States and Canada. The family business continues as a global brand through various members, with current leaders including Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq, Essa Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq and Rashid Farooq. They work with international commercial and charitable organizations.
data.nameEtymology: In English, the family name is often spelled 'Farooq', 'Faruk' or 'Faruq', however in Arabic and Persian the spelling is فاروق.

data.history: The research provided focuses on the ruling Al Maktoum family and the general political history of Dubai, but contains no specific information about the broader Bur Dubai families as a collective group. The timeline shows that Dubai was taken by 'Al bu Falasah clansmen of the Bani Yas' in 1833, with Obaid bin Saeed bin Rashid and Maktoum bin Butti bin Suhail as joint rulers. From 1865, Indian Banians began arriving in Dubai, indicating early merchant settlement patterns. The research mentions various infrastructure developments like Al Fahidi Fort (built around 1787) and Saeed Al Maktoum's House (1896) in what would become the Bur Dubai area. However, the timeline focuses primarily on rulers, major political events, and economic developments rather than providing detailed information about the specific families who settled in Bur Dubai district. The area clearly developed as a commercial center with the establishment of various trading posts and the main port facilities, but specific family genealogies and settlement patterns for Bur Dubai families are not documented in this research.
data.description: The research provided covers Dubai's political timeline and ruling family but contains no specific information about Bur Dubai families as a distinct genealogical or social group.

data.history: Al Barsha is not a family name but rather a district of Dubai, United Arab Emirates, located in western Dubai. The name Al Barsha (Arabic: البرشاء) translates to 'plentiful' or 'small grass' in Arabic, believed to have been named for its once abundant tree cover that gave the surroundings a lush appearance. Prior to development, the area was largely undeveloped desert land with limited infrastructure and sparse residential settlements, with only 1,248 residents recorded in 2000 according to the Dubai Statistics Center. The district was established as a residential area in the early 2000s, with significant transformation beginning around 2005 following the announcement and opening of the Mall of the Emirates. From a scarcely populated locality with minimal roads, Al Barsha rapidly developed into a mixed-use community, with the population growing dramatically to approximately 181,310 residents by 2023. The area is now divided into five sections: Al Barsha 1, Al Barsha 2, Al Barsha 3, Al Barsha South, and Barsha Heights, each serving different residential and commercial purposes.
data.description: Al Barsha is a modern district of Dubai, UAE, established in the early 2000s as a residential and commercial area. The district experienced rapid development from 2005 onwards, transforming from sparse desert settlement to a thriving community of over 181,000 residents by 2023.
data.modernStatus: Al Barsha is a major residential and commercial district in Dubai, home to significant landmarks including Mall of the Emirates, Dubai Miracle Garden, and Dubai Science Park, serving as a mixed-use community with extensive educational, healthcare, and retail infrastructure
data.nameEtymology: Al Barsha (Arabic: البرشاء) translates to 'plentiful' or 'small grass' in Arabic, believed to have been named for its once abundant tree cover that gave the surroundings a lush and complete appearance

data.history: Based on historical records from the early 19th century, the Al Naqbi family was documented as one of the established populations of Khor Fakkan. According to Lorimer's observations at the turn of the 19th century, Khor Fakkan was home to about 150 houses of 'Naqbiyin and Arabs,' totaling approximately 800 people. This indicates that the Al Naqbi (Naqbiyin) constituted a significant portion of the town's population alongside other Arab families. The community's livelihood was primarily based on cultivation and pearling, with the town supporting seven shops, demonstrating a modest but stable economic foundation. The Al Naqbi presence in Khor Fakkan coincided with the area's strategic importance as a port town on the east coast of the UAE, facing the Gulf of Oman.
data.description: The Al Naqbi family was historically documented as one of the primary resident populations of Khor Fakkan in the early 19th century, where they engaged in cultivation and pearling activities. Today, the family includes notable figures such as footballer Abdullah Al-Naqbi.
data.modernStatus: The Al Naqbi family maintains presence in the UAE with notable representation in sports, including footballer Abdullah Al-Naqbi who was born in 1993.

data.history: The Al Qubaysi family represents a prominent lineage with historical connections to Iraq and Syria. Based on the research, the family includes notable academic and religious figures spanning the 20th and 21st centuries. Munira al-Qubaysi (1933-2022) was a Syrian Sufi leader who established significant religious influence. In Iraq, the Al Kubaisi branch produced Basil Al Kubaisi (1934-1973), an Iraqi nationalist activist and academic from an affluent Sunni Muslim family in Baghdad, whose father Raouf Al Kubaisi served as governor of Basra and worked for Hussein bin Ali, King of Hejaz. The family also has documented connections to other prominent Iraqi figures, as evidenced by Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi's wife being identified as Asma Fawzi Mohammed al-Dulaimi, sometimes referred to as 'al-Qubaysi' or 'al-Kubaysi,' indicating marriage connections between these lineages. The Al Qubaysi family demonstrates a pattern of involvement in academic, religious, and political spheres across the Levant and Iraq throughout the modern period.
data.description: The Al Qubaysi is a prominent family with historical roots in Iraq and Syria, known for producing notable religious, academic, and political figures including Syrian Sufi leader Munira al-Qubaysi and Iraqi nationalist academic Basil Al Kubaisi.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains influence through religious leadership in Syria and has historical connections to Iraqi political and academic circles, with documented presence in both countries through the 21st century.

data.history: The Al Osaimi family appears in the research as a politically active family in Kuwait, with documented participation in the country's National Assembly proceedings. Based on the research provided, Nasser Al-Osaimi served as a Member of Parliament who was involved in grilling Justice Minister Khaled Al-Jassar in the Second National Assembly, alongside MPs Suleiman Al-Duwaikh and Hamad Al-Ayyar. Later, Meshari Al-Osaimi served as an MP during the Eighth National Assembly and participated in grilling Second Deputy Prime Minister/Finance Minister Nasser Al-Roudhan. Additionally, Saud Al-Osaimi held the position of Minister of State for Foreign Affairs of Kuwait and represented the Emir of Kuwait at the state funeral of Japanese Emperor Hirohito in 1989, indicating the family's involvement in high-level diplomatic affairs. The family's political engagement spans multiple decades and includes both legislative and executive branches of the Kuwaiti government.
data.description: A Kuwaiti family with documented political involvement in the National Assembly and diplomatic service, including parliamentary and ministerial positions.

data.history: The Al Qadiri family represents a contemporary Kuwaiti family of Iranian descent, established through the artistic and cultural work of Thuraya Al-Baqsami. Born in 1952, Thuraya Al-Baqsami emerged as an internationally acclaimed printmaker, artist, and writer who has significantly contributed to Kuwaiti cultural life. The family gained prominence through her artistic achievements, including her selection by the Kuwaiti Embassy in Berlin in 2002 to design the United Buddy Bear representing Kuwait. Her daughters, Fatima Al Qadiri and Monira Al Qadiri, continue the family's cultural legacy. While the family name 'Al Qadiri' suggests potential historical connections to various Islamic scholarly traditions bearing similar names, there is no documented evidence linking this particular Kuwaiti family to the broader Qadiri Sufi orders or other historical Qadiri lineages mentioned in Islamic scholarship. The family's documented history centers on their Iranian origins and their establishment as prominent cultural figures in modern Kuwait.

data.originStory: The family originated from Iran, with Thuraya Al-Baqsami being born into a Kuwaiti family of Iranian descent in 1952.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence in Kuwait's cultural and artistic community through the work of Thuraya Al-Baqsami as an internationally recognized artist and her daughters' continued contributions to the arts.

data.history:
The Buzzi surname is documented as Italian in origin, with notable bearers spanning multiple centuries and various fields. The earliest recorded figure is Ippolito Buzzi (1562-1634), an Italian sculptor from the Renaissance period. The family name appears prominently in 17th-century Italian arts and architecture, with Carlo Buzzi working as both a painter and architect of the Lombard Baroque school during this period.

Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, the Buzzi name spread internationally while maintaining strong Italian roots. Notable expansion occurred with Arturo Buzzi-Peccia (1854-1943) establishing himself as an Italian-American songwriter, and later generations settling in various countries including Switzerland, Argentina, and the United States. The family has demonstrated particular prominence in creative fields, including architecture, arts, literature, and entertainment.

In modern times, the Buzzi name has diversified across multiple professions and nationalities, from Italian alpine skiing and motorboat racing to Swiss sailing and American entertainment. The family's geographical distribution spans from their Italian origins to Argentina, Switzerland, and the United States, with members achieving recognition in their respective fields throughout the 20th and 21st centuries.
data.description: The Buzzi family is an Italian surname with documented heritage dating back to the 16th century. Family members have achieved prominence across arts, architecture, sports, and entertainment in multiple countries.
data.originStory: The Buzzi family originates from Italy, with the earliest documented member being Ippolito Buzzi, a sculptor active from 1562-1634.
data.modernStatus: The Buzzi name continues through descendants in various countries engaged in diverse professions including sports, entertainment, politics, and business, with no centralized family enterprise documented.

data.history: The Ajami family represents a scholarly and intellectual tradition spanning multiple regions and centuries. The name 'Ajami' derives from the Arabic term meaning 'non-Arab' or 'foreign,' historically used across the Islamic world to refer to non-Arabic languages and peoples. The family's most documented figure is Fouad Ajami (1945-2014), a Lebanese-American academic and political writer who became a prominent voice in Middle Eastern studies. Born in Lebanon during the French Mandate period, Ajami emigrated to the United States in 1963 for higher education, studying at Eastern Oregon College and the University of Washington in Seattle. He later taught at Princeton University before securing the prestigious Majid Khadduri Chair at Johns Hopkins University's School of Advanced International Studies. The Ajami name also appears in the context of the Ajami script tradition in West Africa, where Arabic script was adapted to write local languages like Hausa, demonstrating the historical spread and adaptation of Arabic literacy systems across different cultures and regions.
data.description: The Ajami family includes notable Lebanese-American academic Fouad Ajami and represents both scholarly traditions and the historical use of the term 'Ajami' for non-Arab peoples and adapted Arabic scripts.
data.originStory: The family name originates from the Arabic term 'Ajami' meaning 'non-Arab' or 'foreign,' historically used throughout the Islamic world to distinguish non-Arabic speaking peoples and languages from native Arabs.
data.modernStatus: The family is known primarily through academic and intellectual contributions, particularly in Middle Eastern studies and political commentary, with members residing in the United States and other international locations.
data.nameEtymology: Derives from Arabic 'Ajami' meaning 'non-Arab' or 'foreign,' originally used to refer to non-Arabic languages and peoples throughout the Islamic world

data.history: The Safar family research presents three distinct groups with no apparent connection. The Hungarian Sáfár de Csév family existed from the early 14th to mid-15th century, originating from lesser nobility in Požega County. Stephen I, the family founder, served as a diplomat and castellan under Charles I of Hungary, earning the nickname 'Sáfár' (steward) which became the family surname. The family gained political prominence through strategic marriages and administrative positions until Nicholas II and John II rebelled against Sigismund of Luxemburg in 1405, leading to confiscation of their domains and gradual decline until extinction in 1455. Separately, Hanne Safar (1859-1915) was a notable Syriac Orthodox leader from the Safar family in Midyat during the late Ottoman Empire. He served as mayor and liaison between Christians and Ottoman authorities, receiving honors from Sultan Abdülhamid II. During the 1915 Sayfo genocide, despite initial attempts to protect his community, Safar was eventually executed with his own ceremonial sword. The research also mentions Safar among Persian/Iranian families in Bahrain's Matam Al-Ajam Al-Kabeer, where the Bushehri and Safar families provided two-thirds of donations in the 1890s, but provides no additional details about this branch.
data.description: Multiple unrelated Safar families: a medieval Hungarian noble family (14th-15th centuries), a Syriac Orthodox leadership family in Ottoman Midyat, and a Persian merchant family in Bahrain.
data.originStory: The Hungarian Sáfár family originated from lesser nobility in Požega County, with Stephen I's father Paul supporting Charles Martel's claim to the Hungarian throne. The Midyat Safar family came from Ka'biye village outside Diyarbakir.
data.modernStatus: The Hungarian Sáfár family became extinct in 1455. The Midyat Safar family was destroyed during the 1915 Sayfo. The Bahraini Safar family status is not detailed in the research.
data.nameEtymology: Hungarian 'Sáfár' means 'steward', derived from Stephen I's administrative role as castellan of Visegrád castle.

data.history: The Al Sada family appears to be a prominent Qatari family, though the research does not provide comprehensive details about their historical origins or tribal lineage. The most documented member is Mohammed Saleh Abdullah Al Sada, who has played a significant role in Qatar's energy sector and served as minister of energy and industry. He began his career at Qatar Petroleum in 1983 and held various leadership positions including managing director of RasGas (2006-2011), minister of state for energy and industry affairs (2007-2011), and minister of industry and energy (2011 onward). In 2011, he was appointed chairman of Qatar Petroleum, a position he held until 2018. Notably, in July 2023, Al Sada was elected as chairman of the board of directors for the Russian oil company Rosneft, indicating the family's continued influence in international energy markets.
data.description: A prominent Qatari family known primarily through Mohammed Saleh Al Sada's leadership in Qatar's energy sector and government positions.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant influence in Qatar's energy sector and international oil markets, with Mohammed Saleh Al Sada serving on major corporate boards including Rosneft

data.history: The Al Samahiji family traces its origins to the village of Samaheej on Muharraq Island in Bahrain, where they emerged as a prominent scholarly family in the 17th-18th centuries. The family's most notable figure was Abdullah ibn Salih al-Samahiji (1675-1722), a renowned Shia Islamic scholar and follower of the Akhbari theological school, despite his father being a strict Usuli who opposed Akhbari doctrine. The family's prominence was disrupted by the 1717 Omani invasion of Bahrain, which forced Abdullah al-Samahiji to flee to Isfahan in Iran, where he briefly served as Sheikh ul-Islam under the Safavid administration. He later settled in Behbehan, Iran, where he died in 1722. The family appears to be part of the broader Bahrani scholarly tradition that produced numerous influential Akhbari clerics and theologians during this period. In modern times, the family continues to be represented through figures like Hasan Mohamed Al-Samahiji (born 1991), a professional handball player representing Bahrain internationally, showing the family's evolution from religious scholarship to contemporary sports achievement.
data.description: The Al Samahiji are a Bahrani family from Samaheej village on Muharraq Island, historically known for producing prominent Shia Islamic scholars, particularly in the Akhbari theological tradition during the 17th-18th centuries.
data.originStory: The family originated in the village of Samaheej on Muharraq Island in Bahrain, emerging as part of the scholarly community that flourished during the Safavid period when Bahrain was under Persian influence.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains presence in Bahrain with contemporary members including professional athlete Hasan Al-Samahiji, representing a transition from the family's historical role in Islamic scholarship to modern professional pursuits.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'al-Samahiji' derives from their association with the village of Samaheej on Muharraq Island, Bahrain, following the Arabic naming convention of attributing family names to places of origin.

data.history: Al Sadd Sports Club was established in 1969 by Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah and his relatives in the Al Sadd district of Doha, Qatar. The founding family members excelled in football but refused to join existing clubs, deciding instead to create their own. They consulted with Jassim bin Hamad Al Thani, who was the Minister of Youth and Sports at the time, and received approval to form the club on October 21, 1969. The club's initial popularity was significantly attributed to its affiliation with the Al Attiyah tribe, described as a prominent local tribe, and its connection to the recently dissolved Al Ahrar SC club. Many early players and supporters were remnants of Al Ahrar, which had been permanently shut down in 1967-68 following a mass brawl during a match. The club quickly achieved success, winning their first official Qatar Stars League title in 1973-74 and going on to become the most successful sports club in Qatar with 64 official football championships. Al Sadd achieved international recognition by becoming the first Arab club to win the Asian Club Championship in 1989 and later won the 2011 AFC Champions League, establishing itself as a dominant force in regional football.
data.description: Al Sadd is a Qatari sports club founded in 1969 by the Al Attiyah family in Doha's Al Sadd district. It has become Qatar's most successful sports club with strong tribal connections to the prominent Al Attiyah tribe.
data.originStory: The origin of Al Sadd began with Al Attiyah family members who excelled in football but did not wish to join any existing football clubs. After consulting with the Minister of Youth and Sports Jassim bin Hamad Al Thani, the family, led by Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah, decided to found the club on October 21, 1969, naming it after the Al Sadd district where Ali bin Hamad was born and raised.
data.modernStatus: Al Sadd remains Qatar's most successful sports club, holding a national record of 64 official football championships. The club operates multiple sports teams including football, handball, basketball, volleyball, table tennis, and athletics, with their football team competing in the Qatar Stars League.
data.nameEtymology: The club's name 'Al Sadd' comes from the Al Sadd district of Doha where founder Ali bin Hamad Al Attiyah was born and raised

data.history: The Al Horr family traces its origins to the prominent Shia Islamic scholar Muhammad bin al-Hassan bin Ali bin al-Husayn al-Hurr al-Amili al-Mashghari (1624-1693), commonly known as Al-Hurr Al-Amili. Born in the village of Machghara in the Amil mountains of Lebanon, he came from the Al-Hurr family which was descended from Al-Hurr ibn Yazid al-Riyahi al-Tamimi. During the Safavid era, when the empire was promoting Imami Shi'ism in Iran and bringing in Shi'i scholars from Jabal Amil to replace fleeing Sunni ulama, Al-Hurr Al-Amili migrated to Iran in 1663. He first traveled to Isfahan where he met Muhammad Baqir Majlisi, then settled in Mashhad where he became Shaykh al-Islam at the shrine of the 8th Imam, Ali al-Ridha. He remained in this position until his death in 1693, after which he was succeeded by his brother Ahmad. The Al Horr name appears to derive from 'Al-Hurr' meaning 'the free one' in Arabic, reflecting the family's ancestral connection to Al-Hurr ibn Yazid al-Riyahi al-Tamimi.
data.description: The Al Horr family is descended from the prominent 17th-century Shia scholar Al-Hurr Al-Amili, who migrated from Lebanon to Safavid Iran and became a leading religious authority in Mashhad.
data.originStory: The family originates from Al-Hurr Al-Amili, born in 1624 in Machghara village in the Amil mountains of Lebanon. His family claimed descent from the historical figure Al-Hurr ibn Yazid al-Riyahi al-Tamimi. As part of the Safavid-era migration of Lebanese Shia scholars to Iran, he moved to Mashhad in 1663 where he established the family's presence as religious leaders.
data.tribalOrigin: Al-Tamimi (claimed descent from Al-Hurr ibn Yazid al-Riyahi al-Tamimi)
data.modernStatus: The research does not provide information about the current status or activities of the Al Horr family.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Hurr means 'the free one' in Arabic, derived from the ancestor Al-Hurr ibn Yazid al-Riyahi al-Tamimi

data.history: The Al Zaman family has historical roots connected to the broader Islamic scholarly tradition, with the name appearing in classical Arabic literature through the medieval scholar Badi' al-Zaman al-Hamadani (969-1007 CE), known as 'The Wonder of the Age.' In the modern Gulf context, the Al Zaman family established a presence in Qatar, specifically in the Old Al Ghanim district of Doha. In the early 1950s, coinciding with Qatar's oil exploitation period, the Al Zaman family constructed Bayt Al Zaman, a significant family residence that became a notable landmark in the district. The house served both residential and commercial purposes, with neighborhood women purchasing textiles and household goods directly from the family, functioning as an informal marketplace that allowed residents to avoid traveling to the central Souq Waqif. The residence also featured a makeshift movie theater in its courtyard, which became a community gathering space for local children and neighbors to view films, demonstrating the family's role in local social and cultural life.
data.description: The Al Zaman family established themselves in Qatar's capital Doha in the early 1950s, becoming notable residents of the Old Al Ghanim district where they built an important family home that served both residential and commercial functions in the community.
data.modernStatus: The original Al Zaman family residence, Bayt Al Zaman, remains standing as a detached structure overlooking a public square and Grand Hamad Street, though the original inhabitants have departed and it has since housed various commercial enterprises including an Indian restaurant and migrant worker housing.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Al Zaman' derives from the Arabic word 'zaman' meaning 'time' or 'age,' historically associated with the title 'Badi' al-Zaman' meaning 'Wonder of the Age' used by the classical Arabic scholar al-Hamadani.

timelineEvents.title: Construction of Bayt Al Zaman
timelineEvents.description: The Al Zaman family constructed their family home in Old Al Ghanim district during Qatar's early oil exploitation period

data.history: The Al Yafei family traces its origins to the historic Al-Yafei tribe, which ruled Lower Yafa, a sheikhdom in what was then the British Aden Protectorate (present-day Yemen). The tribe's territory was located in the Abyan Governorate region of southern Yemen. The family name appears in various forms across the Arabian Peninsula, with members found in Yemen, Qatar, and the broader Gulf region. In modern times, the Al Yafei name has appeared in political contexts, notably with Ahmed Ghaleb Nasser al-Rahawi al-Yafei, who served as prime minister of the Houthi-led government in Yemen from August 2024 until his death in an Israeli airstrike in August 2025. The family has also been present in Qatar's sporting community, with Saleh Al-Yazidi representing Qatar at youth football levels, though his family maintains connections to their ancestral homeland in Yafa, Yemen. The Al Yafei are also mentioned among the tribal communities present in the Dhofar Governorate of Oman, indicating the family's dispersal across the southern Arabian Peninsula.
data.description: A family of Yemeni tribal origin descended from the Al-Yafei tribe that historically ruled Lower Yafa in the British Aden Protectorate, with members now found across Yemen, Qatar, and Oman.
data.originStory: The Al Yafei family descends from the Al-Yafei tribe, which ruled the sheikhdom of Lower Yafa in what was historically part of the British Aden Protectorate in southern Yemen. The tribe controlled territory in the Abyan Governorate region.
data.modernStatus: Family members are dispersed across the Arabian Peninsula, with notable political figures in Yemen and athletes representing Qatar, while maintaining ancestral connections to the Yafa region of Yemen.
data.nameEtymology: The name derives from the Al-Yafei tribe, which was associated with the historic territory of Yafa (also spelled Yafei) in southern Yemen.

Despite being from a Sunni background, he worked with the Shia Houthi movement and was killed in an Israeli airstrike.
